- **Free Pass Card**: Loyalty program; enjoy team purchase discount without joining a team (earned after 2 purchases)
- **Brand Black Card**: Encourages reviews by offering branded product discounts (earned after 2-4 reviews)
- **Brand Card**: Promotes branded products; shareable with friends via WeChat to attract them to browse branded products

The Brand Card is strategically important because Pinduoduo has historically been used primarily for non-branded products [1].

### Mini Games
In-app games to drive daily time spent [1]. **Duo Duo Orchard**: "Think of Farmville except now the rewards are real physical goods." Users create and nurture a virtual fruit tree to yield a real box of fruit shipped to their address. Over 11M DAUs [1]. The more you shop, the more water droplets you receive. Team purchases and sharing links yield tools like water cans, decorations, and fertilizer [1]. **DD Bank**: A piggy bank game where users accumulate exchangeable coins [1].

Games tie "progress or 'leveling'" to behaviors driving financial goals: merchants see more volume, consumers have fun and get discounts, Pinduoduo generates revenue [1].

## Personalized Recommendations

Unlike search-based platforms, Pinduoduo is recommendation-based [1]. Using data from social interactions (who a user's friends are, what their favorite categories are, which friends they trust), Pinduoduo makes personalized recommendations. For new customers, they show different categories and observe interactions to create a buying persona [1].

Pinduoduo also personalizes value-for-money: "Based on a user's purchase/browsing history, Pinduoduo knows whether a user values high-cost branded clothing or low-cost non-branded clothing" and only shows matching items [1].

## The Future of Social Commerce

Hariharan argues Pinduoduo's success will be replicated globally [1]. Key insight: "Social commerce does not just mean connecting user accounts to Facebook. It means creating new shopping experiences for buyers and sellers online" [1].

YC companies already pursuing this [1]:
- **[Snackpass](https://www.snackpass.co/)**: Food app for college campuses with social experiences -- students send gifts and hatch virtual pets together by ordering food
- **[Meesho](https://meesho.com/)**: Reseller marketplace in India where customers create microbusinesses selling to friends via WhatsApp

"Companies like Amazon will continue to grow in market share, for specific shopping needs. But it is inevitable that something will rise to fill the e-commerce-social browsing void in the US market" [1].

## References

1. [Pinduoduo and the Rise of Social E-Commerce](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/2z-pinduoduo-and-the-rise-of-social-e-commerce) — [[Anu Hariharan]], Nic Dardenne

title: Boom Supersonic
body:
# Boom Supersonic

Boom Technology (YC W16) is building supersonic passenger airplanes. Founded by [[Blake Scholl]], it represents a case study in starting a hard tech company, selling before having a product, and using YC's structure to accelerate an aerospace startup [1].

## The Opportunity

Fifty years after the Concorde was designed, supersonic travel had not been made mainstream [1]. Scholl frames this as a unique story in technology: "Our phones are better, our computers are better, we're about to have cars that drive themselves and genetically-engineered medicine, and yet we're still flying at 1960 speeds" [1]. The industry is run by optimizers, not founders. Boeing's CEO publicly declared "no more moonshots" [1].

The insight: aviation breakthroughs have always come from founder-led companies [1]. The first airplane (Wright Brothers), the first practical airliner (DC-3), and the first jetliner (De Havilland Comet) were all created by companies run by their founders. The last new commercial aircraft company was founded in 1921. The last founder retired from the industry in 1958 [1].

## Why YC

Scholl was initially skeptical: "Isn't YC for mobile social apps?" [1]. [[Sam Altman]] connected him with hard tech founders who had done YC. They raved about how "YC can help normalize and catalyze ideas that are off the beaten path" [1]. Scholl concluded YC would not help with airplane building but would help with "getting and demonstrating traction and raising capital" [1].

Capital was the biggest risk: "there was a very real risk that we'd never get the money to build anything, and that the company would come and go without ever really starting" [1].

YC gave two pieces of advice: (1) "Get your ass out of stealth mode," and (2) "Go sell some airplanes" [1].

## The Virgin Deal

YC group partners told Scholl: "Show up on demo day with sales or your goose is pretty cooked" [1]. His pipeline included United, Delta, Lufthansa, and Air China -- all impossible to close in 8-9 weeks. "There's no way I'm closing Lufthansa by demo day. Just not going to happen. I'll be lucky if I close Lufthansa in nine years, let alone nine weeks" [1].

Strategy pivot: focus on startup airlines and Virgin [1]. They secured an LOI from a startup doing all-business-class service.

Then, 24 hours before Demo Day Two, Virgin came through: "You can announce the following... We'll take the first 10 airplanes, and we've got options on them. And through Virgin Galactic, we're gonna help you build it" [1]. Scholl "fell off my chair and almost screamed. I had to read the thing three times before I would tell the team" [1].

The result: "$5 billion in LOIs, a record that probably won't be broken soon" [1].

### How They Closed Virgin

1. **Persistence.** YC's biggest sales advice: "Be so persistent that you're sure you're being annoying. And you'll feel like you must be annoying, but when you have that feeling that means you're probably doing it right" [1].

2. **Multiple avenues to the decision-maker.** They had friends in Virgin Galactic supporting them AND astronaut Mark Kelly on their advisory board working Richard Branson directly [1]. "We made sure we had two different avenues to him so that one of them couldn't block us" [1].

3. **Crashed the spaceship rollout.** When Virgin Galactic unveiled their new spaceship in Mojave, Boom engineered a meeting with Branson by telling each side they already had access to the other [1]. "I had to fight my way past the 18-year-olds that were, like, blocking and checking the invite list" [1].

4. **Asked for the right thing.** During the 15-minute breakfast meeting (with Branson and his mom): "We're not asking for your money, we're asking, 'When this flies, do you want a Virgin logo on it?'" [1]. This was key -- making the deal as easy as possible for the other side. See also [[Do Things That Don't Scale]].

5. **Credible deadline.** Demo Day created urgency [1]. Virgin cared about publicity and brand, and "they really wanted to be part of it." Without that deadline, the deal would not have happened [1].

Scholl's summary: "Be fricking persistent, have a deadline, and if you're in a hurry, make the deal as sweet as you possibly can for the other side" [1].

## Hardware at YC: What to Demo

For hardware companies in YC, the challenge is demonstrating progress when the product cannot be finished in three months [1]. Scholl's framework: "Find the thing that you're gonna get done in three months that is progress and looks like progress" [1].

They considered technical milestones ("in our simulations we get a lift-drag ratio that's hitting a critical threshold") but rejected them -- "super meaningful, but no one's gonna understand it or believe it or get excited about it" [1]. They also rejected purely cosmetic demos (a flight simulator) [1].

What they actually did: completed the aerodynamics, built a physical model that showed the innovations, did the high-level jet engine design with a 3D-printed model, and physically brought to Demo Day one of the three actual engines that would power the first airplane [1]. "People, of course, were like, 'OMG, jet engine'" [1]. The engine was real -- "it wasn't bullshit" [1].

## References

1. [On Starting and Scaling Aerospace Company Boom](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/5Q-on-starting-and-scaling-aerospace-company-boom) — [[Blake Scholl]]

title: Three Traits of Great Leaders
related: [[The Second Job of a Startup CEO]]
body:
# Three Traits of Great Leaders

[[Ali Rowghani]] proposes a framework for measuring leadership based on observations from working closely with four leaders he considers extraordinary: [[Ed Catmull]] (Pixar founder), [[Steve Jobs]] (Pixar CEO), John Lasseter (Pixar Chief Creative Officer), and Bob Iger (Disney CEO) [1].

Despite radical differences in style and temperament ("one was an introverted scientist while another was an extroverted artist. One was a college dropout who had founded a company and was infamous for brash behavior while another was a career executive who was exceptionally genteel and diplomatic"), all four built extraordinary trust by excelling in the same three areas [1].

## The Three Traits

### 1. Clarity of Thought and Communication

Great leaders think and communicate clearly [1]. They describe a vision that people find compelling enough to work hard to achieve. "If your employees are confused about your mission and strategy, or do not find it motivating or credible, they will not follow you with the focus and determination necessary to succeed" [1].

**How to improve**: Spend more time thinking about what is truly important for the business. Once crystallized, practice expressing it in simple terms. "Simplicity is vital" [1].

**Bezos example**: Amazon's strategy built on three enduring customer preferences: lower prices, bigger selection, and faster delivery [1]. As [[Jeff Bezos]] said, "You can build a business strategy around the things that are stable in time....when you have something that you know is true, even over the long-term, you can afford to put a lot of energy into it" [1].

**Lutke example**: [[Tobi Lutke]] and his senior team at Shopify spend hundreds of hours preparing for their annual employee Summit [1]. "We want to be a loosely coupled, highly aligned company. The Summit is the main enabler of this because it is a grand sync" [1]. See also [[Mission-to-Metrics]].

Communication preparation becomes increasingly important as the company grows: "your employee base grows more diverse, and fewer of your employees have a personal relationship with you. Hence, they are much less likely to just 'know what you mean'" [1].

### 2. Judgment About People

Great leaders have strong intuition about people, particularly in selecting those they give power and responsibility [1]. "Nothing does more damage to an organization or to the standing of a leader than picking the wrong leaders or failing to correct these mistakes when they happen" [1].

The judgment around the initial hiring or promotion decision matters most: "leaders who fire too many of their own also lose a lot of credibility and trust" [1].

**How to improve**: Gather more data [1]. Meet as many of the best people in a field as possible to sharpen recognition skills. Spend extensive time with executive candidates. Do extensive reference checks asking for examples of behavior showing good judgment and high integrity, "because these traits are hard to test in an interview" [1].

**Uber CTO example**: Thuan Pham was interviewed by CEO Travis Kalanick "for 30 hours straight, one-on-one, over two weeks," including over Skype while Kalanick was traveling [1]. Pham said: "Throughout those 30 hours, I actually forgot it was an interview. It was just like a discussion between two colleagues" [1].

### 3. Personal Integrity and Commitment

Great leaders have exceptional personal integrity and commitment to their mission [1]. Integrity means:
- Standing for something meaningful beyond oneself rather than being motivated by narrow personal interests
- Admitting mistakes rather than acting as though always right
- Receiving critical feedback openly and working to improve
- Avoiding favoritism, conflicts of interest, inappropriate language, and inappropriate work relationships

**The transparency test**: "if your team had full transparency into your private communications and behavior towards employees, would you be embarrassed by anything you have done or said? This is a high bar, but one that great leaders strive to meet" [1].

Beyond integrity, great leaders derive "deep personal meaning and fulfillment from leading people to achieve a mission" [1]. Their commitment translates into high productivity, which becomes the foundation for pushing the organization to do the same [1].

## It All Adds Up to Trust

Building trust is "both a science and an art" [1].

**The science**: Trust grows when leaders' predictions about the future (products to build, investments to make, competitive landscape changes) prove accurate, and when the people they choose to lead prove to be the right ones [1].

**The art**: Trust grows when leaders "say the right thing at the right time, and show empathy and good judgment" [1]. It grows when they stand for ideals bigger than themselves and are honest about what they don't know. "This is why you can't try to copy Steve Jobs or Ed Catmull in your quest to be a great leader. You can only be yourself" [1].

Great leaders treat hard decisions (firing people, saying no, taking responsibility for mistakes) as "opportunities to build trust" [1]. When faced with a difficult challenge, they optimize for trust [1].

"In difficult times, as you evaluate one course of action versus another, ask yourself which path will generate more trust in you as a person and as a leader. Always try to choose that path" [1].

## Measuring Leadership: Survey Questions

Rowghani recommends gathering employee data systematically once a company reaches about 50 people [1]. He provides detailed survey questions organized by trait:

**Clarity of Thought / Communication** [1]:
- CEO: Write down mission, strategy, and key metrics in under 2 minutes. Write down 2-3 themes you consistently emphasize.
- Employees: What is the mission and strategy? What are the most important metrics? How does your work contribute? How often has this changed? What does the CEO consistently emphasize?

**Judgment About People** [1]:
- CEO: Rate each leader you have hired/promoted. Name any you think are wrong for the role.
- Employees: Has the CEO chosen good leaders? Which do you respect? Are there weak ones? Have high performers left, and why?

**Personal Integrity / Commitment** [1]:
- CEO: Have any actions diminished employee confidence in your integrity? Do you ask for feedback and respond to it?
- Employees: Rate the CEO's integrity/moral compass. Does the CEO listen and respond to feedback? Have you seen favoritism or unethical behavior? What motivates the CEO?

## References

1. [How to Measure Leadership](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/3j-how-to-measure-leadership) — [[Ali Rowghani]]

title: Default Alive or Default Dead
body:
# Default Alive or Default Dead

A diagnostic framework introduced by [[Paul Graham]] in his essay "[Default Alive or Default Dead?](http://www.paulgraham.com/aord.html)" [1]. The question: assuming your expenses remain constant and your revenue growth continues on its recent trend, will you make it to profitability on the money you have left?

## The Question

For any startup operating more than 8-9 months, this is Graham's first question [1]. The answer determines the entire conversation. Default alive companies can discuss ambitious plans. Default dead companies need to talk about survival [1].

"The startling thing is how often the founders themselves don't know. Half the founders I talk to don't know whether they're default alive or default dead" [1].

Trevor Blackwell built a [calculator](http://growth.tlb.org/#) to help [1].

## Why Founders Don't Know

Two reasons [1]:

1. **The question changes polarity.** Early on, asking about profitability is meaningless (like asking a 3-year-old about self-support). But as the company matures, it becomes critical. "That kind of switch often takes people by surprise."

2. **They assume they'll raise more money.** "But that assumption is often false, and worse still, the more you depend on it, the falser it becomes."

Graham's recommendation: start asking too early rather than too late. "It's probably not that dangerous to start worrying too early that you're default dead, whereas it's very dangerous to start worrying too late" [1].

## The Fatal Pinch

The terminal condition: **default dead + slow growth + not enough time to fix it** [1]. Founders arrive here by not realizing it's where they're headed.

Graham recommends making hopes explicit: "Say 'We're default dead, but we're counting on investors to save us.' Maybe as you say that, it will set off the same alarms in your head that it does in mine" [1].

## Plan A and Plan B

Even with steep growth (5x/year or more), investors are unreliable [1]. "You can never safely treat fundraising as more than a plan A." Every startup should have a written Plan B:

- Precisely what to do to survive without more funding
- Precisely when to switch to Plan B if Plan A fails

See also [[Financial Survival]].

## The Growth-Spending Disconnect

Graham makes a counterintuitive empirical observation: "In practice there is surprisingly little connection between how much a startup spends and how fast it grows" [1]. Fast growth usually means the product hits a nerve. High spending usually means expensive development, expensive sales, or waste. These are independent [1].

This directly challenges the conventional wisdom that you need to spend to grow. In many cases, the path to growth is [[Do Things That Don't Scale]] or redesigning the product "in the way only founders can" [1].

## Overhiring: The Mechanism

"Hiring too fast is by far the biggest killer of startups that raise money" [1].

Why founders overhire [1]:
1. So much work to do -- naive founders think headcount solves this
2. Successful startups have big teams -- confusing effect for cause
3. Slow growth is painful to face -- hiring distracts from the real problem (unappealing product)

VCs encourage overhiring because "kill-or-cure strategies are optimal for VCs" due to portfolio diversification. "But as a founder your incentives are different. You want above all to survive" [1].

### The Common Death Pattern

1. Make something moderately appealing with decent initial growth [1]
2. Raise a round easily (founders seem smart, idea sounds plausible)
3. Growth is ok but not great (because product is only moderately appealing)
4. Hire aggressively to boost growth
5. Growth never comes (because the problem is the product, not headcount)
6. Rapidly burning runway with high expenses and slow growth
7. Now unappealing to investors
8. Company dies

The fix: address the product problem. "Hiring people is rarely the way to fix that. More often than not it makes it harder" [1].

[[Sam Altman]]: "I think it's roughly correct to say that YC's most successful companies have never been the fastest to hire, and one of the marks of a great founder is being able to resist this urge" [1].

[[Paul Buchheit]]: "A related problem that I see a lot is premature scaling -- founders take a small business that isn't really working (bad unit economics, typically) and then scale it up because they want impressive growth numbers" [1].

## The Airbnb Counter-Example

Airbnb waited 4 months after raising money at the end of YC before hiring their first employee [1]. The founders were terribly overworked, "but they were overworked evolving Airbnb into the astonishingly successful organism it is now" [1].

## References

1. [Default Alive or Default Dead?](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/95-default-alive-or-default-dead) — [[Paul Graham]]

title: Feature Prioritization Frameworks
body:
# Feature Prioritization Frameworks

[[Emmett Shear]] describes three mental frameworks for deciding what to build next, drawn from his experience building [[Justin.tv]] and [[Twitch]] [1]. Startups typically progress through them in order: Built For Me, then Switch To Us, then Three Numbers Matter, as the type of challenge shifts [1].

## The Three Frameworks

### Built For Me

You are the primary exemplar user. Build what you wish the product did [1]. Justin.tv originated this way -- the founders wanted to make a live reality TV show and built the technology for themselves. Twitch started similarly: Shear personally enjoyed the video game streaming part of Justin.tv [1].

**Best when:** You truly love your product, the team is small (hard to scale intuition), and you have genuine access to your own desires [1]. Warning: "'I think I want this because it's what I believe I'm supposed to want' feels subjectively very much like 'I want this'" [1].

**Why it matters strategically:** Many of the biggest startups started this way because "it gives you an automatic advantage against big incumbents who can't use the same method" [1]. Big companies can only replicate this with "hack weeks" that unleash the "Built For Me capacity latent inside any organization" [1].

The one exception at scale: when the CEO is the lead product designer (Steve Jobs at Apple), a big company can escape the dilemma and do "ground-breaking category creation for a product that's great for difficult-to-measure reasons" [1].

### Switch To Us

People are already doing the behavior you want. You want them to do it with your product instead [1].

**Process** [1]:
1. Interview 5-6 dedicated users each from your service, each competing service, and closely-adjacent services (yields ~50 interviews).
2. Categorize responses and score them in a spreadsheet.
3. Look for patterns. Double down on strengths, shore up weaknesses. Target one competitor at a time.
4. Prefer things you can build fast -- "customers don't care how hard something is to build, just how effective it is for their problem."

**The anti-goal: validation.** "Validating an idea you already have is an anti-goal. You can't improve an idea through any amount of validation. The goal of this process is to have better ideas as much as it is to prioritize them" [1].

**Best when:** Your product is important enough that customers have articulate opinions (prosumers, SMBs -- not casual consumers who "may have no idea what it is about SnapChat that they like"), and you are selling "a better mousetrap, rather than inventing the mousetrap category" [1].

**Suggested Interview Questions** [1]:
- What do you like about your current solution?
- What don't you like about it?
- How did you wind up choosing your current solution?
- What were you doing before?
- What would you do if you were the CEO of the current company's product you're using?
- What's the single most annoying thing about your current solution?
- What's the single coolest new thing about your current solution?
- Is there anything that would convince you to switch to using our product immediately if we built it?
- If you had a magic wand and could create any experience you wanted, how would it work?
- What's one thing about your current solution that's surprisingly hard?
- Follow up with "interesting, tell me more" or "why is that?" -- "People know more than they think they do, they just need a good interviewer to draw it out of them."

### Three Numbers Matter

Identify the three most important qualities of your product for your customer, then relentlessly move those metrics [1].

**Examples** [1]:
- **Amazon**: Lower prices, more selection, faster/more reliable shipping
- **Twitch**: Audience size, positive interactions, money (for streamers)

**Why three:** "Empirically, three seems to be the maximum number of focus areas that people can keep in mind consistently. Think about how lists like 'X, Y, and Z' feels natural, but 'W, X, Y, and Z' does not" [1].

A bonus fourth area -- "Stuff You Have To Do" -- covers necessities like PCI compliance and uptime that "should be done, but should not be included in the top level three metrics" [1].

The approach is recursive: each metric can be decomposed into three sub-drivers. Allocate investment by intuition: "maybe it's 33/33/33, maybe it's 50/25/25" [1].

**Best when:** People choose your product for measurable reasons [1]. "Good for making utilitarian things, less good for making games and toys and beautiful UX" [1].

**On operational excellence:** For startups, "it's rare that you can win through pure operational excellence. Usually you need to do just enough operational excellence that it's acceptable to your customers, and then focus on delivering on what your customers really care about" [1].

## Transitions Between Frameworks

Twitch has been running primarily on Three Numbers Matter, but returns to Switch To Us for new customer segments [1]. When launching a new music category, the team talked to artists livestreaming elsewhere to understand their needs. "As the category grows, we have begun to identify the set of metrics that matter most for us and we will begin to transition towards Three Numbers Matter instead" [1].

## The Hard Case

Some products defy all three frameworks: you are building a brand new mousetrap, people choose it for hard-to-measure reasons, and you are not the end user [1]. "It's really really hard!" This constrains big companies more than small ones, since Built For Me does not scale. The result: big companies tend to work on products where drivers are measurable or on incremental innovation [1].

## References

1. [How to Prioritize Features](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8p-how-to-prioritize-features) — [[Emmett Shear]]

title: The Equity Equation
body:
# The Equity Equation

A general formula introduced by [[Paul Graham]] for deciding whether any trade of company stock is worthwhile [1]. The formula applies identically to investor deals, employee grants, and strategic partnerships. ([Original essay](http://www.paulgraham.com/equity.html))

## The Formula

**1/(1 - n)**

Where n is the fraction of the company you're giving up [1]. The deal is a good one if it makes the company worth more than 1/(1-n) times its current value [1].

The logic: if you give up n% of your company, you need the remaining (100-n)% to be worth more than 100% was before [1]. That happens when the company's total value increases by a factor of at least 1/(1-n) [1].

## For Investment

**Example: 50% investor.** If an investor wants half your company, they must at least double your expected outcome [1]. You'd have half of something worth more than twice as much.

**Example: YC at 7%.** 1/(1-0.07) = 1.075 [1]. YC needs to improve your prospects by more than 7.5%. Graham comments: "Does anyone really think we're so useless that in three months we can't improve a startup's prospects by 7.5%?" [1]

**Example: Sequoia at 30%.** 1/(1-0.30) = 1.43 [1]. The deal is worth it if Sequoia improves your outcome by more than 43%. Graham argues this is "an extraordinary bargain" for the average startup because "it would improve the average startup's prospects by more than 43% just to be able to *say* they were funded by Sequoia, even if they never actually got the money" [1]. The reason Sequoia can maintain below-market pricing: "they limit their holdings to leave the founders enough stock to feel the company is still theirs" [1]. The catch: ~6,000 business plans per year, ~20 funded, odds of 1 in 300 [1].

## For Employees

The formula works in reverse [1]. If a new person improves the company's average outcome by a factor of i, they're worth n such that i = 1/(1-n). Solving: **n = (i-1)/i** [1]. See also [[Equity and Compensation]] for practical application.

**Example:** A hacker who'd increase average outcome by 20%. n = (1.2-1)/1.2 = 0.167 [1]. You'd break even giving 16.7%.

But stock is not the only cost [1]. Graham's rule of thumb for converting salary + overhead to stock: multiply the annual rate by about 1.5. Reasoning: if the startup dies, you don't owe future salary; if it grows fast (3x/year), next year's salary comes from next year's (higher) valuation [1].

**Full calculation:** Suppose 50% desired profit margin on the hire [1]. One-third off 16.7% = 11.1% retail price. At $60K/year salary + overhead, x1.5 = $90K stock-equivalent. If valuation is $2M, $90K = 4.5%. Offer: 11.1% - 4.5% = **6.6%** equity [1].

Graham notes: "how important it is for early employees to take little salary. It comes right out of stock that could otherwise be given to them" [1].

## For Any Deal

The meta-principle: "whenever you make any decision involving equity, run it through 1/(1-n) to see if it makes sense. You should always feel richer after trading equity" [1]. If the trade didn't put you net ahead, you shouldn't have done it [1].

## Limitations

Graham is explicit that this is not a precise formula: "I'm not claiming that stock grants can now be reduced to a formula. Ultimately you always have to guess" [1]. There are always other factors -- investor reputation, employee morale, strategic considerations. But the formula provides a foundation for reasoning about what your gut feels or industry tables of typical grants are actually estimating [1].

## Present Valuation

For the "current value" baseline, Graham suggests using the post-money valuation of the last funding round, noting it probably undervalues the company because (a) time has passed and (b) early-round valuations reflect investor contributions beyond cash [1].

## References

1. [The Equity Equation](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8u-the-equity-equation) — [[Paul Graham]]

title: Mission-to-Metrics
related: [[The Second Job of a Startup CEO]]
body:
# Mission-to-Metrics

Mission-to-Metrics is a framework described by [[Ali Rowghani]] for how CEOs create purpose and alignment at a growing company [1]. It consists of three elements: **Mission** (purpose), **Strategy** (direction), and **Metrics** (pace and performance). These provide the essential context that a growing company needs to perform [1].

## Why It Matters

When a startup has fewer than 10 people, alignment is automatic: "Everyone can easily hear what's going on, understand how their work fits into the broader goals, and have a say in every decision" [1]. As the company grows to dozens or hundreds of employees across offices and functions, alignment requires deliberate, structured effort [1].

Rowghani uses a nautical analogy: in Phase 1, the CEO is the lead rower on the boat. In Phase 2, "your job is no longer to row. Instead, it's to define the purpose of the voyage, set the direction of the boat, and measure the pace and performance of a much larger number of rowers" [1]. See also [[Later-Stage Operations]] for [[Sam Altman]]'s perspective on this transition.

## The Framework

### Mission

The mission should feel "ambitious and permanent" [1]. It should find its roots in the reasons the company was founded and should not change very often [1].

### Strategy

Product strategy and go-to-market strategy should be revisited at least twice per year [1]. Rowghani strongly recommends writing strategy in long form. "The CEOs who are most effective in developing and communicating strategy take the time to write their strategy out" [1]. This echoes [[Jeff Bezos]]'s practice at Amazon of requiring 6-page memos for every strategic meeting [1].

### Metrics

A common mistake is equating key metrics with top-line results like revenue or user growth [1]. Top-line results like "increase user growth" are not directly actionable. Instead, dig deeper to understand what drives top-line results [1].

The canonical example: "Facebook famously discovered that connecting a new user to 10 friends within 14 days correlated with retained usage, so they set 'number of new users with 10 friend connections' as the key product metric" [1].

Rowghani: "If you don't know what drives revenue, customer acquisition, or user growth, you aren't likely to be successful anyway" [1].

## The SpaceX Example

Rowghani describes a friend visiting SpaceX's manufacturing floor who asked an employee, "What is your job at SpaceX?" The response [1]:

"The mission of SpaceX is to colonize Mars. In order to colonize Mars, we need to build reusable rockets because it will otherwise be unaffordable for humans to travel to Mars and back. My job is to help design the steering system that enables our rockets to land back on earth. You'll know if I've succeeded if our rockets land on our platform in the Atlantic after launch."

The employee could have simply said he was building a steering system for landing rockets. "Instead, he recited the company's entire 'Mission-to-Metrics' framework. That is alignment" [1].

## Communication

Once Mission-to-Metrics is defined and reviewed by leadership and key employees, the CEO must communicate it constantly [1]. "You have to reiterate the Mission-to-Metrics much more than what feels reasonable, which may run counter to your instinct to be efficient" [1].

The test is not whether employees can repeat it, but "whether they can make good decisions in your absence based on the context you have provided" [1].

[[Tobi Lutke]] of Shopify describes their annual employee Summit as the "main enabler" of alignment: "We spend countless hours preparing because if we communicate well at the Summit, we achieve great alignment by the end. We can then use our weekly townhalls to keep us from drifting too far apart until the next Summit" [1].

## References

1. [The Second Job of a Startup CEO](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/3k-the-second-job-of-a-startup-ceo) — [[Ali Rowghani]]

title: Do Things That Don't Scale
body:
# Do Things That Don't Scale

A framework from [[Paul Graham]]'s essay "[Do Things That Don't Scale](http://www.paulgraham.com/ds.html)" arguing that nearly every successful startup required deliberate, laborious, unscalable effort to get started [1]. The metaphor: car engines once needed hand cranks before electric starters. "Once the engine was going, it would keep going, but there was a separate and laborious process to get it going" [1].

The core insight: "Actually startups take off because the founders make them take off" [1].

## The Techniques

### 1. Recruit Users Manually

"Nearly all startups have to. You can't wait for users to come to you" [1].

**The Collison Installation.** Named after Stripe founders Patrick and John Collison. At YC, when anyone agreed to try Stripe, they wouldn't say "we'll send you a link." They'd say "Right then, give me your laptop" and set them up on the spot [1]. Patrick Collison later described the inflection point: "At some point, there was a very noticeable change in how Stripe felt. It tipped from being this boulder we had to push to being a train car that in fact had its own momentum" [1].

**Airbnb's door-to-door hustle.** The founders went door to door in New York, recruiting hosts and helping them improve listings [1]. They arrived at YC Tuesday dinners with rolly bags, having just flown back from somewhere. "Early on it was so fragile that about 30 days of going out and engaging in person with users made the difference between success and failure" [1].

**Finding the right initial vein.** If you built something for yourself, find your peers. Otherwise, do an untargeted launch, observe who's most enthusiastic, and seek out more like them [1]. Ben Silbermann noticed early Pinterest users were designers, so he recruited at a design blogger conference [1].

### 2. Delight Users Obsessively

Wufoo sent each new user a hand-written thank you note [1]. Graham has "never once seen a startup lured down a blind alley by trying too hard to make their initial users happy" [1].

Three reasons founders don't do this: (1) engineering training doesn't include customer service; (2) they worry it won't scale; (3) they've never experienced such attention from companies themselves [1]. But "that's one advantage of being small: you can provide a level of service no big company can" [1].

Garry Tan warns against the opposite trap: founders who imitate big-company flaws (like indifference to users) to seem "professional" [1].

### 3. The Contained Fire

Start with a deliberately narrow market [1]. "It's like keeping a fire contained at first to get it really hot before adding more logs" [1].

Facebook started Harvard-only. Mark Zuckerberg said creating course lists for each school "made students feel the site was their natural home" [1]. Other startups serve as excellent early adopter companies because they're open to new things and grow fast [1].

### 4. Pulling a Meraki (Hardware)

Assemble your own hardware [1]. Meraki's founders assembled their own routers. Pebble assembled the first several hundred watches by hand. Building yourself means you "can tweak the design faster when you're the factory" [1]. Eric Migicovsky of Pebble: "how valuable it was to source good screws" [1].

### 5. The Consulting Model

For B2B: "pick a single user and act as if they were consultants building something just for that one user" [1]. Safe as long as you're not paid hourly. When you are a product company being extra attentive, customers are grateful. When they pay by the hour, they expect everything [1].

At Viaweb, Graham's team used their own software on behalf of merchants: "we were trying to sell luggage and pens and men's shirts. But in retrospect it was exactly the right thing to do" [1].

### 6. Be Your Software

When user count is small, do by hand what you'll later automate [1]. Stripe delivered "instant" merchant accounts by manually signing users up behind the scenes. "Less frightening than the far more common case of having something automatic that doesn't yet solve anyone's problems" [1].

## What Doesn't Work

**Big launches.** "There may even be an inverse correlation between launch magnitude and success. The only launches I remember are famous flops like the Segway and Google Wave" [1].

**Partnerships.** Six months after a big-company partnership, founders always say: "That was way more work than we expected, and we ended up getting practically nothing out of it" [1].

**Any strategy that omits effort** -- expecting a big launch or a big partner to deliver users -- is "ipso facto suspect" [1].

## Startup Ideas as Vectors

Graham proposes viewing startup ideas as pairs: what you're building + the unscalable thing you'll do to get started [1]. In the best case, both become company DNA: aggressive acquisition stays aggressive, hand-building teaches irreplaceable lessons, and delighting a handful of users creates a culture of delight [1]. See also [[Startup Ideas]].

An idea where "the second component is empty -- an idea where there is nothing you can do to get going, e.g. because you have no way to find users to recruit manually -- is probably a bad idea, at least for those founders" [1].

## References

1. [Do Things That Don't Scale](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/96-do-things-that-don-t-scale) — [[Paul Graham]]

title: Pitch Deck Design
aliases: how to design a pitch deck
body:
# Pitch Deck Design

[[Kevin Hale]] presents a framework for designing pitch decks based on three principles: **legible**, **simple**, and **obvious** [1]. The framework is adapted from a presentation he gives to YC startups before Demo Day. The core insight: "Investors invest in teams not slides. Your slides should make your ideas more clear" [1].

There are probably 100 reasons why a company is great, but people can only remember a few after a short pitch [1]. With each startup, YC identifies the 5-7 most important ideas investors should know. From Prototype Day experience, "you'll be lucky if they can remember even 1 or 2 of those points in the context of 100 other companies also presenting alongside you" [1].

"If you just communicate your points clearly, you'll do better than 99% of startups. Because before anyone can remember, they have to understand" [1].

## The Three Principles

### 1. Legible

"If they can't read it, they won't be able to understand it" [1]. At Demo Day, 500+ people fill the room. Many are older with imperfect eyesight. Legible slides work for everyone [1]:

- Use large type
- Bold text
- A simple font
- Good contrast from the background
- Text at the top (easier to read from the back of the room)

**Shred Video example**: Their Prototype Day slide explaining what the company builds had text too small and too skinny [1]. The text explaining their technology was in "light gray at the bottom of the slide." Their technology (software that automatically edits videos from hours of raw footage into professional-looking output in seconds) was hidden. By Demo Day, they had simplified the description and made the text "large and in charge" [1].

### 2. Simple

Simple and complex share the same word origin: "plex" means braid, twist, or fold [1]. A simple idea is one fold, one idea. A simple slide expresses one idea. "Do not crowd your slides with multiple ideas" [1].

Since there are only 5-7 ideas to communicate, there should be about 5-7 slides [1].

**Afrostream example**: An early slide tried to convey both what they built and who it was for, with too much text including "all the little nuances of their business" [1]. The Demo Day version was "short, sweet, and easy to recollect" [1].

### 3. Obvious

Obvious slides can be understood at a glance [1]. Test: "Show it to a stranger and ask them to tell you what it means. If they don't immediately say your idea, you lose" [1].

Speed of understanding matters because [1]:
1. Demo Day presentations are 2 minutes and 30 seconds
2. "People are easily distracted" -- investors especially so: "If they don't get your point right away, they will check their email."

**How to make things obvious** [1]:

**Make it explicit.** A growth graph alone is not explicit. A growth graph with a bold caption stating "10x growth in 6 months" is explicit. "It's like I put CliffsNotes right on the slide" [1].

**Avoid information distractions.** [Hick's Law](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hick%27s_law): each piece of information added increases comprehension time logarithmically [1]. Diagrams are "like little mazes for ideas" that make the path to the idea too long [1].

Distractions to avoid [1]:
- Too much text
- Excessive explanations and caveats
- Excessive branding per slide
- Photos with no titles or captions
- Animations, transitions
- Memes
- Subtle humor
- Accidental humor

"Don't try to make the slides the thing they'll remember" [1].

### The Exception

Hale allows one exception: when the point is to show complexity or overwhelm the audience [1]. **Magic example**: A slide showing every on-demand service across industries, markets, and verticals on a grid. Normally showing all potential competitors is not ideal, but the logos represent the problem (users need separate accounts for each service) and the opportunity (every service Magic can charge a premium on top of). "It's overwhelming for a reason" [1].

## On Screenshots

Hale describes screenshots as the worst offenders: "Screenshots are almost always illegible, complex, and non-obvious. They break all 3 rules!" [1]. The text in most interfaces is too small (not legible), most interfaces do multiple things (not simple), and most screenshots take longer than a glance to understand (not obvious) [1].

Instead of showing product screenshots, show a simplified version of what the product does [1]. A bulleted list of steps is Hale's preferred technique. **SparkGift example**: A bulleted list explaining product steps was "far better at explaining the ease of use" than any actual UI workflow [1]. **Meadow example**: Explained their product in just two points, which was "far better than any combination of buttons, links and chrome" [1].

This guidance applies equally to screencasts and videos: "Think twice before trying to use either one" [1].

## References

1. [How to Design a Better Pitch Deck](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4T-how-to-design-a-better-pitch-deck) — [[Kevin Hale]]

title: Hiring
related: [[The Second Job of a Startup CEO]]
body:
# Hiring

Hiring is one of the highest-leverage activities for a startup founder. [[Aaron Epstein]] states the principle bluntly: "hiring well is the most important thing you can possibly do to positively impact your business and your team" [3]. [[Harj Taggar]], cofounder of Triplebyte, has observed enormous variance in how effectively companies hire, with offer acceptance rates ranging from 10% to 90% across companies using the same platform [2].

The importance of personal networks for early hiring is one of the most reinforced principles in the YC library, cited by [[Harj Taggar]] [1][2], [[Aaron Epstein]] [3], and [[Ali Rowghani]] [5] across 5 sources. The consensus: for your first hires, exhaust your personal network before turning to any other channel.

## Hiring Your First Engineer

[[Harj Taggar]] describes hiring the first engineer as "incredibly hard" because founders are already stretched thin [1]. The process involves a lot of dead ends and frustration, and "isn't the kind of work that provides you constant dopamine hits."

### Define What You Are Looking For

List all specific criteria for the ideal hire, both technical (engineering skill) and non-technical (productive working relationship) [1]. Mark each candidate against all criteria and "rigorously debate if you think they have enough strengths in some areas to make up for weaknesses in others."

Hiring decisions always involve tradeoffs: quality vs. speed (rejecting solid candidates to wait for the perfect one) or money vs. time (paying above market to close now) [1].

### Sourcing Channels, Ranked by Effectiveness

Taggar ranks sourcing strategies from most to least effective [1]:

**1. Personal Networks (Best)**
The most important source [1]. Hiring someone you have already worked with is optimal because you already know the working relationship. You also have a better chance of convincing someone you know to take the risk of joining.

Taggar is surprised by how often founders do not fully explore their networks [1]: "It's easy to be quick in assuming that none of your friends are available before even asking them." His recommended process:

1. List the best engineers you know, whether you think they are available or not. Go through Facebook and LinkedIn to jog memory.
2. Invite them to lunch or dinner to talk about the startup.
3. Make the ask: "Would you consider joining us?"
4. Whatever they answer, follow up: "If you did join us, which engineers would you most want to hire?"
5. Ask for introductions to those people.
6. Repeat with each introduction.
7. "Repeat ad infinitum. I know public company founders who still do this. Expect to be spending at least a third of your time on this alone."

For the first three engineering hires, focus exclusively on personal network hiring [1]. After that, supplement with other channels to improve team diversity.

**2. Hiring Marketplaces (Second Best)**
Platforms like Triplebyte and Hired operate as two-sided marketplaces [1]. They provide quick access to vetted, skilled engineers who are actively looking. Fees are usually around $25K per experienced engineer. Candidates are speaking with multiple companies, so competition is high. Success depends heavily on pitch quality and process speed.

**3. Generating Inbound**
Post on sites with engineering audiences: [Stack Overflow Jobs](https://stackoverflow.com/jobs), the monthly Hacker News "Who is hiring?" thread, and [AngelList](https://angel.co/recruiting) [1]. Most job sites produce volume, not quality.

Content creation helps: blog about technical choices, unusual stack decisions, or engineering challenges [1]. Taggar advises: "Set a goal of writing an article that's Hacker News front page worthy at least once a month." Interactive content like coding challenges can work but is high-risk.

Make job postings stand out: write in first person as a founder, use informal tone, move away from "corporate boilerplate" [1].

**4. Cold Outreach**
Message engineers on LinkedIn, Hacker News, and GitHub [1]. The challenge is standing out from the overwhelming volume of messages good engineers receive. Personalization is critical. Use tools like Connectifer to find email addresses. Follow up 2-3 times. Expect this approach to take up to 6 months to result in a hire.

**5. Recruiters**
Contract-based recruiters who can send more messages per day, but unlikely to achieve higher response rates than messages from a founder [1]. Train them thoroughly on how to pitch the company: "give them all the information they need, give them a day to prepare and then ask them to pitch it back to you."

**6. Technical Meetups**
High variance [1]. Small, informal, deeply technical meetups can be good for network building but unlikely to produce a quick hire. Only effective if you are either an engineer who can earn technical respect or a highly charismatic personality.

**7. Recruiting Agencies (Worst)**
"Bad adverse selection bias on the candidates they can engage. Most good engineers won't work with them" [1]. Taggar cannot think of a startup that made their first engineering hire through a recruiting agency.

## Convincing Engineers to Accept Offers

Generating an offer is expensive [2]. It takes roughly 5 onsite interviews to produce a single offer (industry standard 20% onsite-to-offer rate), consuming about 30 hours of engineering productivity. Increasing the offer acceptance rate provides direct economic value.

### Speed Matters

Being fast and responsive is a huge advantage startups have over larger companies [2]. Use software to track candidates: a spreadsheet or Airtable initially, then a real ATS like [Lever](http://lever.co/) as you grow. Make clear who reviews and acts on candidates daily.

Taggar warns against rationalizing lost candidates: "It's easy to believe that candidates who drop out because you were slow weren't interested in your company anyway. This is bad reasoning" [2].

### The First Call (25 minutes)

Taggar recommends this agenda [2]:
1. Introduce yourself, the company, and what you work on
2. Ask the candidate what is important to them in their next role (move this earlier, not to the end, so you can tailor your pitch)
3. Ask about skills and experience
4. Pitch the work and why it is an exciting time to join
5. Answer questions and explain next steps

At early-stage companies (<20 people), a founder should always do this call [2]. Taggar recommends the sales/non-technical founder so the technical founder gets uninterrupted building time (per [[Paul Graham]]'s [maker vs manager schedule](http://www.paulgraham.com/makersschedule.html)).

When recruiters handle these calls, set up monthly reporting on call volume and opt-out rates [2]. Assign an engineer to train the recruiter on technical challenges. Provide specific technical questions in multiple-choice format so the recruiter can ask them confidently.

### The Onsite Interview

Standard agenda: welcome (5 min), 4 technical interviews (1 hour each), lunch (1 hour), behavioral/culture interview (45 min), closing Q&A (25 min) [2].

Top three complaints from engineers about bad onsite experiences (from Triplebyte data) [2]:
1. Interviewers being unprepared and knowing nothing about the candidate
2. Interviewers not being familiar with the technical questions they asked
3. Interviewers being determined to prove their own intelligence

Make good interviewing part of company culture [2]. Write an interviewing guide emphasizing being on-time, prepared, and friendly. Create a centralized question bank. Only select the friendliest, most representative engineers as interviewers.

Lunch tip: announce the candidate to the team, have everyone join the lunch table, do a lighthearted icebreaker [2]. "We've had candidates tell us this experience was the deciding factor in joining."

### Making the Offer

**Step One**: Call the same day as the onsite to tell them they will receive an offer [2]. Mention specific reasons from their interview performance. Have multiple team members on the call if possible.

**Step Two**: Call the next day with offer details [2]. Before discussing equity, ask how familiar they are with concepts like stock options or RSUs. Do not overwhelm the unfamiliar. See also [[Equity and Compensation]].

Formal offer letter should include: salary, benefits, signing bonus, health insurance (with policy document links), 401(k), vacation policy, and equity details (total shares, shares outstanding, percentage ownership, exercise price, vesting schedule) [2].

### Closing

Get the entire team involved [2]. Everyone who met the candidate should send a follow-up email with specific reasons they enjoyed the interaction. Describe what the first two weeks would look like. "Nitty gritty details will make the offer feel more real."

Include investors in every engineering offer at early stage [2]. Choose the investor most likely to sell that specific candidate.

Think about the candidate's personal circumstances: other decision makers (spouse, family), relocation concerns [2]. Taggar describes a recruiter who researched career opportunities for a candidate's wife who was reluctant to relocate, gathered contact information and compensation data, and offered to speak with her. "The candidate accepted the offer shortly afterwards."

### Competing Against Big Companies

Arguments that work for startups competing against Facebook and Google [2]:

1. **Learning**: "You learn the fastest by being given real decision making responsibility. Large companies won't give you this kind of responsibility for years."
2. **Career progression**: "Silicon Valley and the technology industry are unique because you can rise up through the ranks of a company faster than anywhere else (e.g. the Chief Product Officer of Facebook ($500bn market cap) is 36 years old)."
3. **Opportunity cost**: "At any point in time there will always be the safe option of working at a public technology company. The names might change but the experience of working at one is fungible."
4. **Mentorship**: If you have experienced engineers who can mentor, make sure to tell candidates [2].

### Exploding Offers

Taggar advises against aggressive deadlines: "good candidates with multiple options will push back" [2]. A softer alternative is an expiring signing bonus: "We want you to take all the time you need to make the right decision. If you are able to make a final decision by X date, we'd be able to offer you a $X,000 signing bonus."

## Hiring Philosophy

[[Aaron Epstein]] encapsulates the core principle: "Hire slow, fire fast. It's the thing everyone says and few have the discipline to do" [3]. Great people want to work with other great people, so resist the temptation to fill positions with mediocre candidates. "You should be thrilled about each new hire" [3].

Epstein also advises hiring for hunger over specific skills: "passion for your mission, business, and team will do more to drive an employee to make a big, long-term impact than any specific skills they may have" [3].

At the senior level, [[Ali Rowghani]] describes how [[Patrick Collison]] of Stripe "made it a point to meet with the 'best-in-the-world' in each field so he could get a sense of what a great candidate looks like" [5]. Executive hiring takes extraordinary time and should be staged rather than done all at once. Rowghani recommends using an executive search firm for the first couple of searches despite high cost [5]. Expect that at least 25% of leadership hires will not work out. See also [[Management]] and [[The Second Job of a Startup CEO]] for more on executive hiring.

## References

1. [How to Hire Your First Engineer](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4H-how-to-hire-your-first-engineer) — [[Harj Taggar]]
2. [Convincing Engineers to Join Your Team](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4G-convincing-engineers-to-join-your-team) — [[Harj Taggar]]
3. [Advice for New Managers](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/2s-advice-for-new-managers) — [[Aaron Epstein]]
4. [YC's Essential Startup Advice](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4D-yc-s-essential-startup-advice) — Y Combinator
5. [The Second Job of a Startup CEO](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/3k-the-second-job-of-a-startup-ceo) — [[Ali Rowghani]]

title: University Spinouts
body:
# University Spinouts

[[Jared Friedman]] provides advice for people who have done scientific research at a university and are considering starting a company to commercialize it [1]. YC has funded more than 75 companies in this situation and conducted a [Bio Tour](https://blog.ycombinator.com/yc-bio-tour-2020/) visiting research universities and talking with hundreds of students and professors in the life sciences [1].

## Deciding Who Should Spin Out

In a typical spin-out, several people worked on the research -- a mix of students, postdocs, and faculty [1]. The first decision: who works on the company and who stays at the university.

### Common Misconceptions

**Misconception 1: You can start a company while continuing your academic career.**

"Your company has little chance of success unless someone who worked on the original research is willing to leave their university role to start this company" [1]. Students graduating soon can wait, but otherwise "at some point you will have to make an intentional decision to leave" [1].

**Misconception 2: You should find a CEO to run the company.**

"Almost always a bad idea" [1]. Great CEOs are scarce and "usually have much better opportunities available to them than running an idea-stage startup with no funding." Most scientists who try this either never find a CEO or settle for a mediocre one. "It's even worse if your university tries to find a CEO for you" [1].

Even with a perfect external CEO: "The people who did the original research will be far more invested in the success of the venture than any outsider. They are also far more qualified to build a company around it because their domain knowledge of the field is much more valuable than whatever general business skills an outside CEO would bring" [1].

A related fallacy: believing the research is done and only commercialization remains. "Usually you find that the thing the market wants is not quite the thing that you've invented, and that more research needs to be done" [1].

**Misconception 3: You need someone with business experience on the founding team.**

"In the first couple of years, there is typically very little 'business' to be done, and whatever business skills you need you will pick up along the way" [1]. Most scientists YC funds have no prior business experience. "People who work in business like to make it sound hard, as if business were like quantum physics, a field that needed to be studied for years to master. The fact is, it's not even close" [1].

**Misconception 4: You should raise money first, then leave the university.**

Scientists often pitch local VCs to validate their idea before leaving [1]. When turned down, they conclude the idea is bad and give up. Actually: "it's just too early at this point to raise money from VCs." Founders typically need 1+ years full-time before a multi-million dollar VC round is feasible [1]. See also [[Fundraising]] and [[Biotech Startup Funding]].

The catch-22: "They are waiting for an investor to take a bet on them before they quit their job. But the investors are waiting for the founders to believe enough in their own company to quit their job!" [1]

In the interim, sustain yourself through savings, government research grants, friends and family, or a small pre-seed round from angels, accelerators, or seed funds [1].

### The Ideal Spin-Out Structure

Two or more people from the lab who did the work leave together as [[Cofounders|cofounders]] [1]. One full-time founder is also acceptable. One of them should be the CEO. Others who want to stay at the university can be "academic cofounders" or "scientific cofounders" and remain helpful, but the full-time founders are the most important [1].

## Deciding When to Spin Out

In early stages, the university is the ideal place: resources for initial experiments, programs like NSF I-Corps for market testing [1]. "At some point, though, that will flip, and being at a university will start to slow you down, because universities are not set up to commercialize technologies" [1].

"The far more common mistake is to wait too long" [1]. Founders delay because leaving is scary: "Academia is a comforting environment. No one is pressuring you to leave." There is always "one more experiment." After leaving, most founders realize "a lot of the work they did in the last year was wasted, because some of their assumptions about what the market wanted were wrong." They also find they move much faster as a company [1].

## Splitting Up Equity

Friedman's framework has two rules [1]:

1. **Full-time founders get equal or nearly equal equity.** (See [[Michael Seibel]]'s essay on [equity splits](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/5x-how-to-split-equity-among-co-founders).) See also [[Equity and Compensation]] and [[The Equity Equation]].

2. **Academic cofounders get much less.** "Founders who will be leaving their job to work on the company full-time should get much more equity than founders who are going to remain in academia. Academic cofounders should typically own no more than 10% unless they are going to continue to be hands-on" [1].

The biggest conceptual mistake: "they think the purpose of allocating equity is to reward past contributions, when actually it's mainly to anticipate future ones" [1].

The marathon analogy: "If you are going to make a new company successful, you will probably have to work on it for 7-10 years post spin-out" [1]. If you are just spinning out, "you might feel like you're halfway done, but actually you are on mile 2 of a 26 mile marathon." The equity split must reflect expected contributions over the whole marathon [1].

One consequence: "your equity split in the new company will not necessarily have any relation to your seniority within the original academic team" [1]. Junior lab members who leave may get much more equity than their former boss who stays. "This can be an awkward conversation, but it's entirely sensible" [1].

## Negotiating with Tech Transfer Offices

The technology transfer office (TTO) manages IP licensing from the university to the startup [1].

Historically, TTOs had "a well-deserved bad reputation" for being slow, bureaucratic, and imposing onerous terms that "strangled the very companies they were trying to create" [1]. Things have improved. Universities in major startup hubs (Harvard, MIT, Stanford) now give reasonable terms, though they still take too long. Some universities use "[express license agreements](https://ctl.cornell.edu/entrepreneurs/fasttrack/)" requiring little negotiation [1].

### Four Key Terms

1. **Equity.** University gets equity in the company. 3-5% is typical. "Above 10% will cause problems" [1].
2. **Royalty.** Percentage of revenue/profits to the university. "If this is too high, it can affect the viability of the company to raise money and operate." Aim for zero; keep it low if zero is not possible [1].
3. **License scope.** Whether the license is exclusive or non-exclusive, and to which fields it applies [1].
4. **Milestone requirements.** Conditions the company must meet to keep the license (e.g., raising funding, hiring, filing patents) [1].

Friedman emphasizes that these negotiations should be completed before or shortly after spinning out, not delayed indefinitely [1].

## References

1. [How to Spin Your Scientific Research Out of a University and Into a Startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8f-how-to-spin-your-scientific-research-out-of-a-university-and-into-a-startup) — [[Jared Friedman]]

title: Management
related: [[The Second Job of a Startup CEO]]
body:
# Management

Management in startups is a learned skill, not an innate one. Many first-time founders begin managing teams with zero management experience. [[Aaron Epstein]], founder of [Creative Market](https://creativemarket.com/), describes the fundamental tension: "you have to create both a product that people love and a company where people want to work *at the same time*" [1]. The stakes are high because "people leave managers, not companies" [1].

## Advice for New Managers

[[Aaron Epstein]] provides a comprehensive set of principles for founders learning to manage for the first time [1].

### Delegation and Growth

**Delegate.** Founders are used to doing everything, and shedding responsibilities feels scary [1]. But "you can't scale yourself and focus on the highest leverage opportunities until you get things off your plate." Signal to delegate: thinking you will have to work especially late or cannot get to what you need to in a given day.

**Create growth opportunities.** "Great people often care about personal growth way more than money or any other tangible benefit you could offer them" [1]. Delegating responsibilities creates opportunities for people to learn and grow. See also [[Hiring]] for more on what motivates great employees.

**Invest in your stars.** Retention is easier than acquisition: "it's much easier to keep an existing star employee happy than find a new star" [1].

### Communication and Direction

**Share the big picture.** People need to understand how their work fits into the business [1]. "It's easy to take it for granted that your team is clear on this, but this is one of those things where you want to constantly make that connection."

**Repeat yourself often.** "You should feel like you sound like a broken record about the things that are important to your company -- your mission, your vision, your KPIs" [1]. The test: "every single person on your team will be able to explain them to a complete stranger exactly how you would've said it." See also [[Mission-to-Metrics]] for the framework on cascading purpose through an organization.

**You are in charge.** It can be uncomfortable telling people what to do for the first time [1]. "But you are the leader, and everyone on your team will be looking to you for direction and guidance, so own it."

**Focus on the What, not the How.** Communicate what success looks like rather than micromanaging the process [1]. "It's a way for you to 'be in the room' when the work gets done without micromanaging the work."

**Verbalize your thoughts.** "Simply saying things like 'I'm disappointed in this work' is extremely powerful" [1]. People are not mind-readers.

### Accountability and Feedback

**Set deadlines and hold people accountable.** When giving a task, ask "by when?", then add a calendar note to follow up [1]. Give direct feedback when standards or deadlines are not met.

**Deflect all credit and absorb all blame.** Use "I" for failures, "we" (or specific names) for successes [1]. "A little recognition goes a long way."

**Show your work.** Explain the reasoning behind controversial or difficult decisions so the team understands the rationale [1].

### People Management

**Manage for the employee.** Epstein's favorite interview question for manager candidates: "How would you describe your management style?" The best answer: "it depends on the employee" [1]. Each person has a unique combination of experience, motivations, and personality.

**Understand that everyone is different.** "Just because you may be an achiever that's self-motivated to get things done doesn't mean that everyone else on your team is" [1].

**Have consistent 1-on-1s every week.** These are for the benefit of employees; let them drive the agenda [1]. Ask open-ended questions. "Go out of your way to keep from canceling or rescheduling them whenever possible."

**Include your team in decision-making.** Define the process upfront: committee decision, someone else owns it, or gathering feedback for the CEO's final call [1]. "Even if your team disagrees with the final call, by including them in the process they will better understand the decision."

**A little professional tension is healthy.** Differing perspectives make teams and products better, but "if the tension moves from professional to personal, then you need to take action to eliminate it quickly" [1].

### Onboarding and Development

**Set your team up for success.** "It's unfair to expect even the best people to hit the ground running on day one without being properly onboarded" [1].

**Your trust should be earned.** Work more closely with new team members and frequently review their work until they have proven themselves [1].

**Ease the maker to manager transition.** An outstanding individual contributor may not be an outstanding manager immediately [1]. During the transition, their instinct will be to take on both roles: "spending 75% effort on making and 75% effort on managing -- a 150% workload resulting in less than 100% output in each area." Put structure in place to let them focus on managing.

**Create a career path.** Every 6 months, ask employees where they want to be in 2-3 years [1]. Help them build a plan.

## The CEO's Evolving Role

[[Ali Rowghani]] describes three phases of a startup CEO's job in [[The Second Job of a Startup CEO]] [2]:

- **Phase 1**: Build a product users love. The CEO is the "Doer-in-Chief."
- **Phase 2**: Build a company to maximize the opportunity. The CEO is the "Company-Builder-in-Chief."
- **Phase 3**: Harvest profits from the core business to invest in transformative new products.

Phase 2 typically begins around 20-25 employees and ends at 400-500 [2]. The transition from Phase 1 to Phase 2 is the critical inflection point. "It's hard to stop coding, designing product specs, and interacting with customers on a daily basis." But "once your startup reaches 20-30 people, you'll have to spend more time leading. And since time is finite, the only way to lead more is do less" [2].

[[David Rusenko]], co-founder of Weebly, captured the end state: "Often, the first time I find out about a product feature is reading about it on our blog. It shocks most founders to hear this, but I know I've done my job well because I've yet to see a feature that was built poorly" [2].

### Three Tasks CEOs Cannot Delegate

Rowghani identifies three tasks unique to the CEO [2] (see also [[Mission-to-Metrics]] and [[Three Traits of Great Leaders]]):

**1. Hiring a Leadership Team**
Only the CEO can make final hiring decisions for VPs and C-suite [2]. "You can't hire by compromise, looking for someone who everyone around you likes." Expect to spend 50% of your time on this. Expect at least 25% of leadership hires to not work out. "Most startup CEOs take too long to fire their first executive." See also [[Hiring]] for tactical advice.

Senior executives should be managed to longer-term outputs, not weekly milestones [2]. It is also the CEO's job to acclimate new executives to culture and insist they build relationships across the organization before changing things.

**2. Creating Purpose and Alignment**
See [[Mission-to-Metrics]] [2]. When a startup has fewer than 10 people, alignment is automatic. At 50+ people hiring across offices and functions, it requires deliberate work. The CEO defines Mission (purpose), Strategy (direction), and Metrics (pace and performance).

**3. Nurturing Company Culture**
Culture is "defined by the way people treat one another in a company" [2]. It begins forming on day two. Unlike [[Mission-to-Metrics]], culture is not solely the CEO's job: "CEOs should encourage co-founders and early employees to work together to codify a set of values and behavioral norms that feel authentic and aspirational to everyone" [2].

Rowghani uses the Pixar example: during the making of Toy Story 2 in 1999, with seven months to release, the creative leadership decided the film was not working [2]. Disney argued it was too late to start over. Pixar refused, tore up the story and rewrote it from scratch, pushing the studio to the brink of collapse. After completion, they closed the studio for two months to let everyone recuperate. [[Ed Catmull]] called it "the most intense and important period in the studio's history" and said it defined two core cultural tenets: "We couldn't be a studio that produced both great work and average work" and "we had to take care of our people if we were going to ask such sacrifices from them" [2].

### The Measure of Success

[[Steve Jobs]] said during a management meeting at Pixar: "When I'm at my best, 50% of my time is unscheduled. That's the time I use to think, drop in on the people I want to speak with, and let my curiosity roam. It's my time to be creative. Without this free time, I would never be able to stay ahead of the company. To lead a company, you've always got to be two steps ahead. There's no way to lead a company from behind" [2].

Reaching the point of having significant unscheduled think time is the clearest sign of success for a Phase 2 CEO [2].

## Measuring Leadership

[[Ali Rowghani]] proposes a framework for measuring leadership based on his experience working closely with [[Ed Catmull]], [[Steve Jobs]], John Lasseter, and Bob Iger at Pixar/Disney [3]. See [[Three Traits of Great Leaders]] for the full framework.

The core insight: despite radically different styles, all four leaders built extraordinary trust by excelling in the same three areas: clarity of thought and communication, judgment about people, and personal integrity and commitment [3]. "You cannot be a great leader without them because you cannot build trust without them."

## References

1. [Advice for New Managers](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/2s-advice-for-new-managers) — [[Aaron Epstein]]
2. [The Second Job of a Startup CEO](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/3k-the-second-job-of-a-startup-ceo) — [[Ali Rowghani]]
3. [How to Measure Leadership](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/3j-how-to-measure-leadership) — [[Ali Rowghani]]

title: Founder Mindset
body:
# Founder Mindset

The most important quality in a startup founder is not intelligence, technical skill, or business acumen. It is determination. [[Paul Graham]] has spent years refining this view, ultimately distilling the ideal founder quality into two words: **relentlessly resourceful** [1].

Determination as the primary founder quality is one of the most reinforced principles in the YC library, cited by [[Paul Graham]] across 6 separate sources [1][2][3][4][5][6], and independently confirmed by [[Aaron King]] [7] and [[Sam Altman]] [5]. Graham returns to it in nearly every essay about founders: "The most important quality in a startup founder is determination. Not intelligence -- determination" [2].

## Relentlessly Resourceful

In his essay "Be Relentlessly Resourceful," Graham arrives at this phrase after years of trying to articulate what makes a good founder [1]. He started from the opposite direction -- the word "hapless," which implies passivity, being "battered by circumstances" rather than having your way with the world. The antonym of hapless is relentlessly resourceful.

Why both words matter [1]:

- **Relentless** alone is insufficient. Pure relentlessness works only in "a few mostly uninteresting domains." In any interesting domain, obstacles are novel -- "you don't know initially how hard they are; you don't know whether you're about to plow through a block of foam or granite."
- **Resourceful** alone lacks drive. You need to keep trying new things, adapting constantly.

The best metaphor is a running back: "A good running back is not merely determined, but flexible as well. They want to get downfield, but they adapt their plans on the fly" [1]. In "The Hardest Lessons," Graham extends this: "A successful running back doesn't just put his head down and try to run through people. He improvises: if someone appears in front of him, he runs around them; if someone tries to grab him, he spins out of their grip; he'll even run in the wrong direction briefly if that will help. The one thing he'll never do is stand still" [2].

Graham distinguishes this from stubbornness, which he calls "a disastrous quality in a startup" [2]. The founder must be "determined, but flexible."

Can this quality be taught? Graham's answer, after four years at YC: "Yes, surprisingly often it can. Not to everyone, but to many people" [1]. Young people who have been under the thumb of authority may have a latent ability that "only needs to be brought out." Being relentlessly resourceful "is definitely not the recipe for success in big companies, or in most schools" [1].

The phrase works as both a diagnostic and a rallying cry. "'Make something people want' is the destination, but 'Be relentlessly resourceful' is how you get there" [1].

## Determination Over Intelligence

Graham is explicit about the primacy of determination over intelligence. In "The Hardest Lessons for Startups to Learn," he writes: "The most important quality in a startup founder is determination. Not intelligence -- determination" [2].

He grounds this in personal experience: "I'd like to believe Viaweb succeeded because we were smart, not merely determined." But his years in New York convinced him otherwise: "You can lose quite a lot in the brains department and it won't kill you. But lose even a little bit in the commitment department, and that will kill you very rapidly" [2].

VCs get this wrong, Graham argues [3]. "Time after time VCs invest in startups founded by eminent professors. This may work in biotech, where a lot of startups simply commercialize existing research, but in software you want to invest in students, not professors. Microsoft, Yahoo, and Google were all founded by people who dropped out of school to do it."

In "Why to Not Not Start a Startup," Graham adds nuance: you need to be moderately smart, but "starting a startup just doesn't require that much intelligence" [3]. If you worry you're not smart enough, that very worry suggests you are. The Silicon Valley "cult of smartness" distorts reality. "If you don't think you're smart enough to start a startup doing something technically difficult, just write enterprise software" [3].

## Commitment as Self-Fulfilling Prophecy

Graham argues that commitment creates its own momentum [2]. If people sense you're ambivalent, "they won't give you much attention." Conversely, if you're clearly determined to stick around, "people will pay attention to you, because odds are they'll have to deal with you later. You're a local, not just a tourist."

This applies directly to [[Fundraising]] and acquisitions [2]. Acquirers who think you'll stick around no matter what are more likely to buy you, because "if they don't and you stick around, you'll probably grow, your price will go up, and they'll be left wishing they'd bought you earlier." Investors are driven by fear of missing out (FOMO): "If you make it clear you're going to succeed no matter what, and the only reason you need them is to make it happen a little faster, you're much more likely to get money" [2].

YC has seen founders who treat their startup as a three-month experiment, planning to continue only if "something great" happens (meaning acquisition or large investment) [2]. "But if this is your attitude, 'something great' is very unlikely to happen to you." You can't fake commitment: "The only way to convince everyone that you're ready to fight to the death is actually to be ready to" [2].

## Shielding Optimism

Startup founders must be optimistic -- "they wouldn't do it otherwise" [2]. But Graham argues optimism must be shielded like the core of a nuclear reactor: "as a source of power that's also very dangerous."

The shielding principle: "Be optimistic about what you can do, but assume the worst about machines and other people" [2]. This is especially critical with deals.
