Graham's hypnotic suggestion: when someone says "we want to invest in you" or "we want to acquire you," the phrase "don't get your hopes up" should automatically appear in your head. "Just continue running your company as if this deal didn't exist. Nothing is more likely to make it close" [2].

The practical danger of unshielded optimism isn't disappointment -- it's dependence [2]. "If someone says they want to invest in you, there's a natural tendency to stop looking for other investors." If you lean against something that falls over, it takes you with it. Keep looking even after getting a positive signal: "Even if you ultimately do the first deal, it will be to your advantage to have kept looking, because you'll get better terms" [2].

## What Drives the Most Successful Founders

In "Billionaires Build," Graham identifies the deepest motivator of billion-dollar founders: genuine interest in what they're building, not money or status [5]. "One thing few people realize about billionaires is that all of them could have stopped sooner. They could have gotten acquired, or found someone else to run the company. Many founders do. The ones who become really rich are the ones who keep working."

Startup motivations are usually mixed: desire to make money, desire to seem cool, genuine interest in the problem, and unwillingness to work for someone else [5]. "The last two are more powerful motivators than the first two." Founders motivated purely by money take the first acquisition offer. Those motivated by seeming cool "rapidly discover that there are much less painful ways of seeming cool" [5].

The Airbnb founders exemplified this [5]. During their YC interview, "the whole interview showed that they cared. They weren't doing this just for the money, or because startups were cool... They had discovered an interesting new idea, and they just couldn't let it go."

## Intellectual Honesty About External Conditions

[[Aaron King]] of Snapdocs (YC W14) demonstrates a related founder quality: intellectual honesty about market conditions [7]. During COVID's housing boom, King told his team: "This industry works in cycles. We're in an up cycle now and that's great. There's going to be a down cycle... We should not overly congratulate ourselves for the current situation, just as we shouldn't be too hard on ourselves when we're fighting through an inevitable downturn."

King's approach to market cycles: be selective about which changes to react to [7]. When well-resourced with momentum, ignore market downturns and focus on market share growth. When conditions change, scale back ruthlessly. "In a startup, you're always making hard decisions based on insufficient information." See also [[Financial Survival]] for more on navigating market cycles.

## Fear the Right Things

Graham observes in "The Hardest Lessons" that startups often fear the wrong things [2]. Visible disasters -- a founder quitting, a patent threat, servers crashing, a deal falling through -- are par for the course. "They won't kill you unless you let them."

Big company competitors are not the main threat either: "The people at Google are smart, but no smarter than you; they're not as motivated, because Google is not going to go out of business if this one product fails; and even at Google they have a lot of bureaucracy to slow them down" [2]. The real threat is other startups you don't know about.

But even competitors are not the biggest danger. "Way more startups hose themselves than get crushed by competitors" [2]. The three main self-inflicted wounds: internal disputes, inertia, and ignoring users. The worst recipe for a startup: "a couple of founders who have some great idea they know everyone is going to love, and that's what they're going to build, no matter what" [2].

As Richard Feynman said, "the imagination of nature is greater than the imagination of man" [2]. You'll find more interesting things by looking at the world than by pure thinking. "No idea for a product could ever be so clever as the ones you can discover by smashing a beam of prototypes into a beam of users."

## References

1. [Be Relentlessly Resourceful](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/94-be-relentlessly-resourceful) — [[Paul Graham]]
2. [The Hardest Lessons for Startups to Learn](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8w-the-hardest-lessons-for-startups-to-learn) — [[Paul Graham]]
3. [Why to Not Not Start a Startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8x-why-to-not-not-start-a-startup) — [[Paul Graham]]
4. [Before the Startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8y-before-the-startup) — [[Paul Graham]]
5. [Billionaires Build](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/9M-billionaires-build) — [[Paul Graham]]
6. [A Student's Guide to Startups](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/93-a-student-s-guide-to-startups) — [[Paul Graham]]
7. [Snapdocs' Aaron King on Navigating Market Cycles](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Gu-snapdocs-aaron-king-on-navigating-market-cycles) — [[Aaron King]]

title: Growth Teams
body:
# Growth Teams

A growth team is a cross-functional unit dedicated to driving user acquisition, activation, and retention through structured experimentation. [[Anu Hariharan]] and [[Gustaf Alstromer]] compiled best practices from 25 growth experts at companies including Facebook, Airbnb, Uber, Stitch Fix, Square, Slack, and Instagram [1].

## When to Invest in Growth

The cardinal rule: do not build a growth team before proving you can retain customers. Investing in growth before retention is proven is "a great way to waste money, resources, and jeopardize the future of your company" [1]. If retention is not yet solved, a growth approach can be applied to retention itself -- for example, Stitch Fix hired a retention-focused PM to run experiments improving retention before investing in new customer acquisition [1].

### The Retention Checklist

Before building a growth team, companies should verify retention through four steps [1]:

1. **Pick the right metrics.** Choose a leading indicator of revenue and repeat behavior, not a vanity metric like app downloads. For marketplaces, measure both supply and demand sides. For Airbnb: "rebook rate" (% of customers that rebook) and "nights booked per user" on the demand side; "active hosts" and "bookings per active host" on the supply side. For Uber: rider retention and trips per active rider; driver retention and trips per active driver [1].

2. **Pick the right cohort period.** Typically a day, week, or month. Airbnb measures retention annually because travel is low-velocity. Uber measures monthly and weekly because ride frequency is high [1].

3. **Identify an initial user action.** 100% of the install base takes some action that is a leading indicator for revenue. For Airbnb, this is booking a room for at least one night. For Uber, a first ride [1].

4. **Identify a follow-on action.** Calculate the percentage still engaging in subsequent periods [1].

### Good Retention vs. Bad Retention

Three tests determine if retention is good [1]:

1. **Stable long-term retention** parallel to the x-axis (after an expected initial dip).
2. **In line with vertical benchmarks:**

| Vertical | Period | Long Term Period | Long Term Target | Median |
|----------|--------|-----------------|-----------------|--------|
| Social Network | Monthly | Month 12 | 45%-65% | 55% |
| On-Demand | Monthly | Month 12 | 20%-30% | 22% |
| Travel | Annual | Year 2 | 20%-35% | 29% |
| E-commerce | Monthly | Month 12 | 10%-25% | 16% |
| Subscription | Monthly | Month 12 | 25%-35% | 33% |

3. **Newer cohorts perform better** than older cohorts, implying product improvement [1].

Airbnb is cited as an example of great retention: stable long-term retention, each new cohort performing better than the previous one, and rates exceeding competitors in the travel vertical [1].

## Building the Growth Team

### Composition and Timing

The typical Year 1 Growth Team: **1 Growth-focused PM + 2-3 Growth Engineers + 1-2 Growth Data Scientists** [1].

When to hire [1]:
- Most companies made their first growth hire when they had about **15 engineers** on the product team.
- The Growth PM is typically the **3rd or 4th PM** on the team.
- "The most common mistake CEOs make is waiting too long before they hire a growth-focused PM."

The first hires are "magnets" for subsequent hiring and scaling. 100% of experts emphasized this [1]. Eric Colson, former VP of data science at Netflix and early Stitch Fix hire, attracted many accomplished data scientists to Stitch Fix by reputation alone [1].

Facebook pioneered the growth team model. Its first team had 3 people, formed when Facebook had approximately 50 million MAU with roughly flat month-on-month growth. The growth team "became a key driver of Facebook's rapid expansion to 2 billion monthly active users" [1].

### The Ideal Growth PM

1. **Data-oriented** (must-have): "The scariest day is when numbers are down, the second scariest day is when numbers are up and you don't know why" [1].
2. **Prior growth experience**: Should have worked at a company focused on driving growth in a competitive space (e-commerce, dating, gaming, social). More than 90% of experts cited this. Not from Google or Apple, as "those teams didn't scale based on competitive growth strategies" [1].
3. **Former startup founder** (bonus): 60% of growth experts interviewed were former founders. "People who've started companies tend to be able to think independently, are comfortable with taking risks, and have high levels of perseverance" [1].
4. **Existing PM** (bonus): 40% of growth leads were already PMs at their company. Facebook and Slack both promoted internal PMs. Airbnb and Uber hired externally [1].

### The Ideal Growth Engineer

1. **Self-starter**: Proactive about generating hypotheses and running experiments [1].
2. **Comfortable losing code**: "A large amount of work won't make it into the final product" [1].
3. **OK [[Do Things That Don't Scale|doing things that don't scale]]**: Engineers with 2-4 years experience may fit better than veterans who "train toward rigid requirements and roadmaps" [1].
4. **Great communicator**: Comfortable working cross-functionally with design, copywriting, data [1].

### The Ideal Growth Data Scientist

1. **Fluency with experimental design**: Can estimate sample sizes, correct for multiple comparisons [1].
2. **Coding ability**: Growth requires more data preparation than other data science roles. Test with live pair coding on data cleanup in Python or R [1].
3. **Great communication**: Must articulate both what can and cannot be validly deduced from experiments, and make the persuasive case for investing in growth initiatives [1].

## The Growth Team's First Year

### 1. Set an Absolute Goal and Define Key Metrics

Goals must be absolute numbers, not percentage changes. Casey Winters (former Growth Product Lead at Pinterest) [wrote about this](http://caseyaccidental.com/growth-goals) [1]. Example: "achieve 5M first-time room nights this year" -- not "improve conversion rates by 10%" [1].

Break the absolute goal into sub-goals. Jonathan Hsu (Social Capital, Facebook's early growth team) shared the [growth accounting equation](https://medium.com/swlh/diligence-at-social-capital-part-1-accounting-for-user-growth-4a8a449fddfc) [1]:

- **Airbnb**: Room Nights = Room nights from new users + Room nights from existing users
- **Facebook**: MAU = New MAU + Retained MAU + Resurrected MAU

For marketplaces, set absolute goals for both supply and demand sides. Set the goal halfway between "sandbagging" and "too hard to achieve." 100% of experts said the CEO must be aligned when setting the goal, and it must be communicated to the entire company [1].

### 2. Identify Growth Channels

Use existing user behavior to identify channels. Aatif Awan (LinkedIn Head of Growth) [outlined this framework](https://www.slideshare.net/tractionconf/aatif-awan-head-of-growth-linkedin-growth-hacking-is-dead-long-live-growth) [1]:

| User Behavior | Channels to Explore | Example Companies |
|--------------|-------------------|-------------------|
| Need to connect with another user to use product | Product itself | Facebook, PayPal, Slack |
| Existing users talk about product | Referrals, Community | Uber, Airbnb, Dropbox |
| Use search to find solution | SEO, SEM | Airbnb |
| Look for expert inspiration | Affiliate bloggers, Pinterest, Partnerships, Content | Stitch Fix, Glossier, Intercom |
| High LTV users | Paid acquisition (social, search, native, offline) | Airbnb, Expedia, Uber |

Most products find 1-2 relevant channels early. Approximately 70% of experts said referrals were the top channel in the first year [1].

### 3. Establish Systems and Tools

Four essential elements [1]:
- **Clean data set** to track key metrics
- **Segmentation tools** for granular customer and activity understanding
- **Rigorous experiment dashboard** tracking results and statistical significance
- **Peer review process** for discussing findings

At scale, a company typically runs **1 experiment per growth engineer per week** [1]. 100% of experts eventually built their own internal tools, though initial tools like [Mixpanel](https://mixpanel.com/), [Optimizely](https://www.optimizely.com/), [Superset](http://airbnb.io/projects/superset/), and [Chartio](https://chartio.com) are common starting points [1].

Growth teams that run 100+ experiments per year report that only about a third are positive. The 20-30% success rate is intentional -- the exercise encourages engineers to take more risks [1].

A common heuristic: "Don't test things you wouldn't ship to everybody" [1].

### 4. Establish User Research

Data alone cannot answer all questions. The first 100M users will look very different from the second 100M [1]. Key practices:
- Solicit real-time feedback from users
- Use tools like [Inspectlet](https://www.inspectlet.com/) to track UX
- Meet users outside your first core market (e.g., outside San Francisco)
- Pay attention to international cultural nuances (e.g., "people in Japan do not like to post photos of people without their permission")
- Document every use case [1]

### 5. Continue to Iterate

Tools, processes, and systems evolve at scale. The experiment dashboard, for example, may take many iterations to formalize -- one expert cited it was only formalized after having 25-30 growth engineers [1].

## Where Should the Growth Team Sit?

This is the biggest source of debate [1]:

- **Separate department** (Facebook model): Growth is its own department, Head of Growth reports to CEO. Facebook's rationale: "if they didn't assign sole responsibility for growth of MAUs, then no one would own it" [1].
- **Merged with product** (Uber, Airbnb, Slack model): Started separate but merged because "growth is not about just looking at data to drive insight" -- the team also makes product changes. Head of Growth reports to Head of Product [1].
- **Within marketing** (least favored): "The general sentiment about this approach is that the line of reporting is a bit rooted in the past" [1].

Regardless of structure, at scale a growth team can exceed 100 people, composed roughly of: 10% PMs, 50% Engineers, 10-15% Data Scientists, 10% Product Marketing, 10-15% Designers, ~5% Researchers [1].

## References

1. [How to Set Up, Hire, and Scale a Growth Strategy and Team](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/59-how-to-set-up-hire-and-scale-a-growth-strategy-and-team) — [[Anu Hariharan]], Gustaf Alstromer

title: Board Governance
related: [[The Second Job of a Startup CEO]]
body:
# Board Governance

Board management is one of the most anxiety-producing aspects of scaling a startup. [[Anu Hariharan]] notes that "this is the first time the founder/CEO is subject to external supervision, and the board has powers that include the firing of the CEO and the senior management" [1]. Despite this, boards, when composed and managed well, are a significant resource: "they will challenge you to make better decisions but are on your side" [1].

## Purpose of a Board

The board's main role is to guide major decisions: hiring/firing senior management, approving corporate actions (compensation, stock options, budget), and offering strategic guidance [1]. Every Delaware corporation must have a board once it starts operating. Initially the board is just the founders, functioning as a technical body for approvals. "Setting up a board" typically means adding outside directors after raising money [1].

## Board Composition

- **Series A and B investors** typically join the board [1]. Later-stage growth investors usually do not ask for seats.
- **Early stage**: 3-5 seats. **Public company**: 7-9 seats [1].
- The #1 attribute for a board member is **trust**, built only through time [1]. [[Anu Hariharan]] recommends getting to know VC partners 6-9 months before [[Fundraising]]. Mathilde Colin of Front [documented her approach](https://medium.com/@collinmathilde/front-series-a-deck-f2e2775a419b) [1].

**Independent directors**: Many founders are adding independents earlier [1]. Examples from YC companies:
- Justin Kan (Atrium) added [[Michael Seibel]], a former co-founder, to his Series B board
- Brex founders (Henrique and Pedro) added Victor Lazarte, a founder from Brazil who had known them since age 16
- Faire founders added Brian Grassadonia (Square Cash lead, their former manager at Square)

These choices reflect CEOs' desire for "deeply trusted independent board directors" [1]. As Hariharan notes: "this does not mean you add your friend to your board. You need someone you really trust, who is 100% independent, and has the ability to help both you and your team as you scale."

For board structure and control terms, see [[Fundraising]] (Series A section) and [[Series A Term Sheet]] [2].

## Managing Board Meetings

### Meeting Schedule

- **Series A stage**: Informal meetings (like 1:1s), bi-weekly to bi-monthly [1]
- **Series B+**: Quarterly, all meetings scheduled a year in advance, in-person only [1]

### Agenda and Length (~3 hours)

The most common mistake is treating board meetings as status updates [1]. The best CEOs allocate only the first 45 minutes for highlights/lowlights and KPIs, then use the remaining time for 1-2 strategic deep dives.

**Example agenda from Gusto** (shared with permission) [1]:
- Highlights / Lowlights (10 mins)
- Performance / KPIs (50 mins)
- Strategic topic 1: 3-Year Strategic Plan (45 mins)
- Strategic topic 2: Engineering Org -- Current and Future (45 mins)
- Closed Session (30 mins: approvals, sensitive topics, feedback)

This structure means 1/3 of time on business state, 2/3 on strategic issues [1]. Hariharan recommends identifying and aligning on key KPIs early to "train" board members to process them quickly.

### Board Deck

The deck is a tool for discussion, not an end in itself [1]. Bryan Schrier from Sequoia has written on [preparing a board deck](https://www.sequoiacap.com/article/preparing-a-board-deck/).

**Example structure from Gusto** (30-50 pages) [1]:
- Highlights / Lowlights: 1 page
- Performance / KPIs: 15-20 pages
- Strategic topic 1 (5-10 pages): options considered, decision framework, product roadmap, areas not pursuing, known vs. unknown risks, economic value assessment
- Strategic topic 2 (5-10 pages): current org, hiring scale, future org (18 months), talent assessment, retention/development/recruitment plan
- Appendix: health of organization (10 slides), [[Hiring]] metrics, close rates, employee pulse survey

### Pre-Meeting Preparation

**Gusto's approach** [1]: Send a pre-read board deck at least a week in advance. Collect questions from each board member in a Google Doc three days before. Answer all questions in the doc by the morning of the meeting. The in-meeting discussion then focuses on the two strategic topics.

**Brex's timeline** (managed by co-founders, CFO, and Chief of Staff) [1]:
- T-28 days: Identify two strategic topics, align with executive team
- T-18 days: Outline topics, write/collect content, share draft with exec team
- T-11 days: Execs comment/edit, finance team finalizes including formatting
- T-7 days: Final review, distribute deck to board
- T-4 days: Board submits questions (particularly on KPIs)
- T-2 days: Brex consolidates and answers all questions
- T-0: Set expectations upfront for which sections need most time

### Executive Involvement

Two approaches to involving the executive team [1]:
1. Ask all executives to attend the entire meeting (risk: meeting becomes a presentation, not a discussion)
2. Specific executives join for their agenda item (~45 minutes each) (preferred by Hariharan)

The second approach achieves: a meatier discussion, executives hearing board feedback directly, and more time for the CEO and board to discuss sensitive matters privately [1].

General counsel and/or outside counsel should attend all board meetings to capture notes and address attorney-client privilege matters [1].

### Monthly Updates

In addition to quarterly meetings, send a 2-page monthly update email [1]. Typical contents: last month's performance summary, [[Hiring]] update, burning issues, specific asks for help.

**Brex** always includes two specific asks: introductions to business partners/customers, and help with key open roles [1]. **GitLab** asks for introductions to specific individuals at potential customer companies and publicly applauds investors who have helped, "putting pressure on the entire board to do the same" [1].

Monthly updates accomplish three things [1]: (1) board members go above and beyond to help with specific asks, (2) the board arrives better prepared for in-person meetings, (3) it forces the CEO to step back and reflect.

As [[Jeff Bezos]] has [argued about memos](https://www.inc.com/carmine-gallo/jeff-bezos-bans-powerpoint-in-meetings-his-replacement-is-brilliant.html), "written narrative is more compelling in sharing ideas than bullet points and slides" [1].

### Board Letters / Memos

Some CEOs write longer reflections [1]:
- **Peter Reinhardt (Segment)** writes a 3-5 page memo focusing on highlights, lowlights, where he would like to spend more time vs. where he is spending time, and his own strengths and weaknesses as CEO
- **Faire** uses written memos for strategic priorities during board meetings, including decision frameworks, reasoning, uncertainties, and blind spots

## Navigating Board Issues

### Mixed Messages

Conflicting viewpoints are a good thing [1]. The board's job is to push on strategy and sharpen thinking. If conflict makes the conversation unproductive, take it offline to 1:1 or small group meetings.

A YC CEO resolved conflicting board opinions about product priorities by creating a decision-making framework (economic value, resources, cash needs, competitive threat) [1]. "Coming up with the framework also made it quite clear which path was the right one." A clear framework is especially helpful "when you decide to go against the board's advice."

Key principle: "While board members provide important input all CEOs should seriously consider, the CEO has the authority to make the best decision on behalf of the company" [1]. See also [[Management]] for more on CEO decision-making.

### Asking Board Members for Help

Get to know where different board members can be most helpful and follow up directly outside board meetings [1]. Do not waste time updating every board member 1:1 on every topic or before every meeting. "Allow your board members to challenge you and the team during the board meetings and encourage conflicting views" [1].

### Balancing Helpfulness vs. Control

"Be cautious of board members who are trying to make decisions for you" [1]. Great board members ask questions to flesh out CEO thinking rather than stating opinions.

Hariharan cites Micky Malka of Ribbit Capital (Brex board member): when asked "What should our typical credit loss rate be?", Malka would share examples of 10 startups with their credit loss rate trajectories, pros and cons, but refrain from giving one definitive answer [1]. "He empowers the founders to make the decision."

### Problem Board Members

If a board member is unhelpful: meet 1:1 with specific examples from past meetings [1]. Focus on the issue, not the person.

If a board member works around the CEO (e.g., having direct meetings with senior management without the CEO's knowledge): one YC CEO resolved this by having 1:1 conversations with other board members, gathering feedback, and directly approaching the disruptive member [1]. It took about 6 months to resolve. The CEO then worked with the rest of the board to set up a framework for when and how the board can intervene.

### Removing Board Members

Options [1]: (1) approach someone senior at the board member's firm to seek a replacement (will burn bridges), or (2) add more board members (independent or via new investment rounds) to dilute the influence. If the company is performing well, the firm will work hard to find a replacement. If not, leverage is limited.

Critical self-check: "If all your board members are echoing the same feedback and you are not listening, then the problem is likely you" [1]. Some CEOs bring an executive coach to 1-2 board meetings to get feedback on dynamics.

## References

1. [How to Create and Manage a Board](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/3w-how-to-create-and-manage-a-board) — [[Anu Hariharan]]
2. [A Standard and Clean Series A Term Sheet](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4P-a-standard-and-clean-series-a-term-sheet) — [[Jason Kwon]], [[Aaron Harris]]

title: Later-Stage Operations
body:
# Later-Stage Operations

[[Sam Altman]] addresses the challenges that startups face 12-24 months in, after finding [[Product-Market Fit]]. The main job shifts from building a great product to building a great company [1].

## Management at Scale

Before PMF, everyone can report to the founder. This works well up to roughly 20 employees. "But what works well at 20 employees can be disastrous at 40" [1].

The initial structure need not be complex: "all you need is for every employee to know who their manager is and for everyone to have exactly one manager" [1]. Managers should know their direct reports. Cluster people into teams that make sense. "Clarity and simplicity are important -- I wouldn't try to innovate on management structure" [1].

### Common Management Failures

**Senior hires:** In the early days, hiring senior people is usually a mistake. As the company scales, "it's actually valuable to add senior people/executives to the team that have built companies before" [1].

**Not delegating:** Founders take on too much personally. "As your company keeps growing, you become stretched thin and things get done poorly" [1].

**Delegating poorly:** Having employees do grunt work while making all decisions yourself does not work for scalability or employee effectiveness. "Empower employees to make their own decisions. Let them know how you think about a problem, but let them own the decision process" [1].

Altman advises: delegate most things, especially what you do not want to do. Keep one thing you enjoy or where your involvement adds disproportionate value [1].

**Personal organization:** Developing a system to track tasks and follow-ups becomes crucial as you become a manager [1].

**Communication at scale:** Beyond a certain size, one-on-one conversations with everyone are impossible. "Learn to communicate in clear, succinct writing, and share it with everyone. It's one of the highest leverage things you can do, and it scales to thousands of people" [1]. Hold all-hands meetings at least monthly and maintain one-on-ones with direct reports [1].

## HR and People Operations

Most founders ignore HR initially. Continuing to ignore it at later stages is "a huge mistake" [1]. Good HR means three things: clear management structure, a way for people to raise workplace concerns, and pathways for career evolution [1].

### Performance and Compensation

Performance feedback "can be light, but it should happen frequently" [1]. It should be clear how performance ties to compensation. "People talk, and they will eventually find out co-workers' compensation levels. If it's all over the place, it can be a complete disaster" [1]. **Compensation bands** solve this: a mid-level engineer knows their range, a senior engineer knows theirs [1].

### Equity

"YC company data suggests that most successful companies give out a lot of equity" [1]. Altman recommends planning to give away **3-5% of the company each year for ten years** from founding. This is a lot of stock but keeps people motivated and aligned [1]. See also [[Equity and Compensation]].

Critical: **refresher grants** for employees approaching vesting cliffs. "You never want an employee in a place where they start thinking about leaving because they've vested three out of their four years. Always stay in front of people's vesting schedules" [1]. Altman links to Wealthfront's post on [refresher grants](https://blog.wealthfront.com/how-long-should-you-stay-at-your-job/) [1].

### Other HR Practices

- **Build a diverse team.** "You want unity of vision, but diversity of backgrounds is good. Myopic culture is typically less effective" [1].
- **Announce offers internally.** Up to a few hundred employees, announce potential offers on an internal mailing list. "Very often someone in the company will know something good or bad about the prospective employee" [1].
- **Structure onboarding.** "Have a program in place to ramp up new employees" [1].
- **Build paths for early employees.** The original 10-15 may not fit executive roles, but "they're probably loved by their peers, and very productive." Talk to them directly about career progression [1].
- **Monitor burnout.** "Building a company really is a marathon and you cannot sustain people working 100 hour weeks in perpetuity" [1].
- **Hire a full-time recruiter** once you have PMF. "Getting the best people is arguably the most important thing to do" [1].

## Company Productivity

"Building a company that is able to innovate repeatedly is the hardest thing in business" [1]. Companies become unproductive because people are not on the same page or do not understand priorities [1].

### The Alignment Test

Altman describes a test he gives struggling companies: "If I asked 10 employees what the top three goals are for the company, would they say the same thing?" [1]. 100% of founders say yes. "I'll then go and do it, and 100 percent of the time no two employees say the same thing" [1]. See also [[Mission-to-Metrics]] for a systematic approach to creating alignment.

The fix: reiterate the roadmap and goals frequently. Have management meetings with direct reports every week. Hold company all-hands at least monthly covering the roadmap, three-month trajectory, and how it connects to longer-term goals [1].

### Operational Tips

- **Do offsites.** Take best people outside the workspace where "everyone has time to just talk and think through the bigger picture" [1].
- **Get legal docs in order.** Assign someone to collect every agreement the company has ever signed [1].
- **Start FP&A early.** "If you have someone build a really great model of the business, you can optimize and understand how things are working at a level that most people totally miss" [1]. Worth doing well before hundreds of employees.
- **Hire a fundraiser.** A full-time fundraiser after the B round, preparing for the C round, "you'll almost certainly get better results than if you hire an investment banker later on" and pay less in fees and dilution [1].
- **Pay attention to unit economics.** "Sooner rather than later, you need to figure out how to make more money from each user than you spend" [1]. Most great companies historically have had good unit economics soon after beginning to monetize.
- **Watch runway.** "Don't ever get down to just a couple months of cash in the bank" [1]. See also [[Default Alive or Default Dead]].

## Founder Psychology at Later Stages

"The highs are higher but the lows keep getting lower" [1]. As success grows, "people start rooting against you. Journalists and people on the internet will find ways to pick apart what you're doing" [1]. Altman advises learning to ignore the haters early [1].

Long-term commitment is a massive advantage: "Have a strategy that assumes you will work on your company for the next 10 years" [1]. Very few founders make this commitment [1].

Take vacation. "We often see founders that go three or four years without ever taking a real vacation" [1]. Burnout is inevitable without rest [1].

Do not lose focus. "Focus is what made you successful in the first place" [1]. Talking to potential acquirers is "another very dangerous" distraction [1].

## Navigating Success: The Airplane vs. Spaceship Framework

Altman uses a metaphor (from David Weiden): starting a startup is like being pushed off a cliff with a bag of aerospace parts. You hope they are spaceship parts. Sometimes they are, sometimes they are nothing, and a reasonable amount of the time "it turns out you have the parts for an airplane" [2].

An "airplane" is defined as a company with profitability within reach, on track to be worth $100 million with several more years of hard work. "This is nothing to be ashamed of -- quite the opposite" [2].

### The Investor Misalignment Problem

Founders and investors often diverge at this point. "Many startup investors are looking for spaceships -- airplanes don't matter much to them, though they matter quite a bit to the founders" [2]. Existing investors will usually take almost any risk to see if an airplane can become a spaceship [2].

Altman advises: "Be frank and realistic with your existing investors; you will eventually persuade them that a small multiple is better than a loss" [2]. The best investors understand and may even "treat you extra-generously in the hopes of funding your next company" [2].

### The Valuation Trap

"Raising too much money or raising money at too high a valuation can severely limit your optionality" [2]. Founders who know they have an airplane but convince investors it might be a spaceship cause enormous heartache and "often precludes your opportunity for a good acquisition later" [2].

The answer: "Don't raise a lot of money at a high price when you think the company is probably not going to increase another 10x in value and you'd be pretty happy selling the company soon" [2]. This may require finding a path to profitability with current cash [2].

### Positioning for Acquisition

"Companies get bought, not sold" [2]. Forcing an acquisition is very difficult and "nearly impossible if you haven't built something legitimately valuable" [2]. Begin developing relationships with potential acquirers early -- "it usually takes a long time, and personal relationships usually help" [2].

"Silicon Valley is filled with people who had one or several mid-success companies before eventually hitting it out of the park" [2].

## Dealmaking

12-24 months in, business development starts to matter (assuming a great product exists) [1]. Key principles:
- Develop personal connections. "No one wants to feel like they're being used transactionally" [1].
- Set up competitive situations. "You get good terms and deals done when you have a competitive situation and you stay persistent" [1].
- Ask for what you want. "Most of the time you won't get laughed out of the room, and you might actually get it" [1].

## References

1. [Later-Stage Advice for Startups](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4q-later-stage-advice-for-startups) — [[Sam Altman]]
2. [Navigating Success for Your Startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4s-navigating-success-for-your-startup) — [[Sam Altman]]

title: Enterprise Sales
body:
# Enterprise Sales

[[Ryan Junee]] reframes enterprise sales for technical founders: "being a great enterprise salesperson requires the same skill set as being a great hacker -- you just need to reframe how you think about sales" [1]. Where a computer system is a complex network of hardware and software, a large enterprise is a complex network of people and processes that can be understood and influenced in the same way [1].

This advice focuses on early-stage companies in the "wild west" stage -- those without an established repeatable sales process, selling $1M+ deals to Fortune 500 companies. Junee assumes approximate [[Product-Market Fit]] [1].

## The Enterprise as a System to Hack

"Enterprise sales involves understanding and influencing the components (people) of the system in order to produce the desired behavior (buying your product.)" [1]. Every system (company) is different, requiring a systematic approach.

At mature companies, sales processes are scripted -- what Junee calls "[script kiddies](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Script_kiddie)" rather than hackers [1]. Early-stage startup sales is fundamentally different: you are exploring uncharted systems. "The more complex the system, the more satisfaction the hacker feels when he or she pwns it" [1].

Enterprise systems are harder than computer systems for two reasons: the components (people) "don't always operate according to structured, rational rules" because they have emotions, motivations, hopes, and fears; and the feedback cycle is much slower, meaning "you need to be much more methodical in your approach -- brute force is generally not an option" [1].

## Key Players in the Enterprise System

### Champion
Your ally inside the target company. "He or she will have deep familiarity with the pain your product solves, and will hope that your product is the solution. When you give your pitch, you will find the champion nodding along and completing your sentences for you" [1].

Champions may become so excited they want to quit and join your company (Junee says this has happened to him multiple times) [1]. They act as if they are your employees -- "your guy/gal on the inside." Find them early and build strong relationships. Line-level champions are useful, but you also need director-level and above [1].

"These people are gold" [1].

### Detractor
The opposite of a champion. "Maybe it threatens their job. Maybe they are already invested in a competing product. Maybe they built the solution that is currently being used" [1]. Strategy: identify them early, stay off their radar, and rally enough support from champions to overpower them [1].

### IT
Two types: risk-avoidant (perceives change as threatening) and forward-thinking (embraces change). The former is far more common. "It's usually a good strategy to avoid talking to IT" unless your product's users are in IT [1]. IT meetings can lead you astray -- Junee was "burned by this in the past, building features that IT asked for in order to get a deal done only to discover when putting the product in front of actual users that it wasn't what they wanted" [1].

Strategy: avoid IT until required for security reviews and approvals. By that time, the business units should be "pulling" you so strongly that IT cannot slow things down for too long [1].

### Procurement
Once a decision maker agrees to buy, procurement negotiates the deal. "Usually these folks are incentivized to get the lowest price possible" [1]. Strategy: "make sure you are prepared to 'give' them something -- once they extract their pound of flesh they will get out of the way" [1]. This is why much enterprise software has a "ridiculously high 'list price', but discounting by 80% or more is standard practice" [1].

### Legal
Their weapon is exhaustion: "sending back endless redlines over clauses in the contract that in reality won't have much impact" [1]. Strategy: decide upfront what you really care about, give in on what does not matter, then have "a frank and authentic discussion" listing those things. Combine this with the champion pushing legal from the inside to hurry [1].

### Finance
May not interact directly, but important to understand their motivations: budget cycles (they may prefer to pay more upfront to use this quarter's budget), and capex vs. opex preferences (lump sum purchase vs. monthly license fees), which affects your pricing model and sales team compensation structure [1].

## Tactical Advice for Hacking the Enterprise

**Uncover the motivations of every player.** "Map out the people in your target company, draw a diagram" [1]. Spend significant time listening and learning before attempting to influence the system, like a hacker exploring how a system works before exploiting it [1].

**Build strong relationships with champions.** "When doing a large enterprise deal it will fall through before it closes, maybe more than once" [1]. You need champions who will revive deals. "You should be able to text them any time of night and ask for their help" [1].

**Hire the right type of salespeople.** "Optimize for skills and raw characteristics over experience" [1]. The most common mistake is hiring for the wrong stage -- "hiring a 'coin operated' script kiddie salesperson out of a large company when you really need a hacker" [1]. Look for people "comfortable with uncertainty, who can learn quickly, and have strong intuitive and intellectual intelligence" [1].

**Keep your map front and center.** Constantly re-evaluate the people, their motivations, and their interactions. "Position your product in the right way to the right people at the right time" [1].

**Engage on many fronts.** Create pairs of relationships: your executives with their executives, your engineers with their IT and security, your product people with their business unit leaders. Always have one person (usually the account executive) coordinating all interactions [1].

**Instrument your process.** Measure your sales process as you would use "a packet sniffer or debugger when hacking" [1].

**Companies are people.** "Decisions are made by people, and people don't always act rationally" [1]. Friendships lead to partnerships, and friendships transcend employers. "By nurturing these relationships they will pay off across multiple companies" [1].

## References

1. [Enterprise Sales for Hackers](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4m-enterprise-sales-for-hackers) — [[Ryan Junee]]

title: Becoming a Founder
body:
# Becoming a Founder

The decision to start a startup is one of the most consequential career choices a person can make. [[Paul Graham]] has written extensively about who should start startups, when, and what holds people back [1][2][3][4]. The YC perspective is that far more people are capable of founding startups than actually do it, but it is not for everyone.

The advice to "just learn" and build expertise before starting a company is one of the most reinforced principles in the YC library, cited by [[Paul Graham]] [2][3], [[Michael Seibel]] [7][8], and the YC team [5] across 12 sources. The consensus: the best preparation for founding a startup is becoming genuinely expert at something, not studying entrepreneurship.

## Who Should Start a Startup

[[Michael Seibel]] recommends asking yourself three questions [8]:

1. Do I like being the underdog?
2. Do I seek hard challenges most people shy away from?
3. Do I thrive taking personal responsibility for success or failure?

"If you answer yes three times, then maybe starting a startup is for you," Seibel writes [8]. "I cannot promise that doing a tech startup will make you rich but I can promise that it is one of the most challenging things you can choose to do."

Graham's test is more specific. In "Be Relentlessly Resourceful," he defines the ideal founder quality: someone who is [[relentlessly resourceful|Founder Mindset]] -- not merely determined, but flexibly determined, adapting on the fly like a running back [6]. "If you want to know whether you're the right sort of person to start a startup, ask yourself whether you're relentlessly resourceful."

In "Billionaires Build," Graham identifies the deepest motivator of the most successful founders: genuine interest in what they're building [3]. "What keeps them working is the same thing that keeps anyone else working when they could stop if they wanted to: that there's nothing else they'd rather do." The Airbnb founders' Obama-and-McCain-themed breakfast cereal story was "the single most important factor" in YC's decision to fund them -- not because of the cereal, but because it showed they were resourceful, determined, and could work together [3].

## The Counterintuitive Nature of Startups

Graham's "Before the Startup" lecture lays out six counterintuitive truths [2]:

1. **Startups are counterintuitive.** Trust your instincts about people, but not about startup strategy. "If someone seems slippery, or bogus, or a jerk, don't ignore it" [2].

2. **You don't need to be an expert on startups.** "The way to succeed in a startup is not to be an expert on startups, but to be an expert on your users and the problem you're solving for them" [2]. Mark Zuckerberg succeeded despite being "a complete noob at startups."

3. **Gaming the system stops working.** "There is no boss to trick, only users" [2]. Faking can fool investors for one or two rounds, but the company is ultimately doomed.

4. **Startups are all-consuming.** "If your startup succeeds, it will take over your life for a long time: for several years at the very least, maybe for a decade, maybe for the rest of your working life" [2]. Larry Page at 25 started running and hasn't stopped. YC-funded founders of big successes all say: "It never gets any easier."

5. **You can't predict if you're up to it.** "For the past 9 years it was my job to predict whether people would have what it took to start successful startups... the answer is: not much" [2]. The swaggering recruits are no more likely to be tough than the quiet ones.

6. **The way to get [[Startup Ideas]] is not to try to think of them.** (See [[Startup Ideas]].) [2]

## Why to Not Not Start a Startup

In his essay by that title, Graham systematically examines 16 common reasons people don't start startups and identifies which are legitimate and which are bogus [1].

### Reasons that are mostly bogus:

**Too young.** The most successful YC founder at the time, [[Sam Altman]], was 19 [1]. Two tests for whether someone is old enough: (1) Do they still have the "kid flake reflex" -- crying "I can't do it" instead of powering through? (2) When challenged, do they submit or rebel (both childish) or calmly ask "Really? Why do you think so?" (adult response) [1].

**Too inexperienced.** "The best way to get experience if you're 21 is to start a startup" [1]. The Kiko founders started right out of college, failed, but became "extremely formidable" within a year. "There is no way they'd have grown so much if they'd spent that year working at Microsoft, or even Google" [1].

**Not smart enough.** "If you're smart enough to worry that you might not be smart enough to start a startup, you probably are" [1]. Silicon Valley has a cult of smartness, but in reality most startups succeed through effort, not brilliance. "If you don't think you're smart enough to start a startup doing something technically difficult, just write enterprise software" [1].

**Know nothing about business.** "You don't need to know anything about business to start a startup. The initial focus should be the product" [1]. Revenue is not what acquirers buy startups for; they buy strategic value, meaning something people want.

**No idea.** YC weights the team far more than the idea [1]. "We're so sure the founders are more important than the initial idea that we're going to try something new: we're going to let people apply with no idea at all." The average YC startup changes 70% of its idea in the first three months.

**No room for more startups.** This is a fallacy [1]. "No one claims there's any limit on the number of people who can work for salary at 1000-person companies. Why should there be any limit on the number who can work for equity at 5-person companies?"

**Fear of uncertainty.** "If you start a startup, it will probably fail" [1]. But no one will blame you, and managers at big companies all said they'd prefer to hire someone who'd tried a startup and failed over someone who spent the same time at a big company.

**Parents want you to be a doctor.** "Treat their opinions like feature requests. Even if your only goal is to please them, the way to do that is not simply to give them what they ask for" [1].

### Reasons that are legitimate:

**Family to support.** Graham is explicit: "I wouldn't advise anyone with a family to start a startup" [1]. The alternative is a consulting business gradually converted into a product business, or joining an existing startup as an early employee (roughly "1/n^2 founder, where n is your employee number").

**Not ready for commitment.** A successful startup consumes at least three or four years [1]. If you value freedom and flexibility above all else, that's a perfectly legitimate reason to wait.

**Need for structure.** "If you're one of these people, you probably shouldn't start a startup. In fact, you probably shouldn't even go to work for one" [1].

**Not determined enough.** Determination is "probably the single best predictor of success" [1]. But Graham notes that "most hackers probably underestimate their determination. I've seen a lot become visibly more determined as they get used to running a startup." See also [[Founder Mindset]].

## The Optimal Time to Start

Graham's recommended sweet spot for startup founders is the **mid-twenties** [3]. Here's why:

**Advantages of youth (from "A Student's Guide to Startups")** [3]:

1. **Stamina.** "I can't think of any successful startups whose founders worked 9 to 5."
2. **Poverty.** Living cheaply is critically important. "Nearly every startup that fails, fails by running out of money." [[Steve Wozniak]] designed the Apple II to use a TV as a monitor because he couldn't afford a real display. As Wozniak put it: "All the best things that I did at Apple came from (a) not having money and (b) not having done it before, ever." See also [[Financial Survival]].
3. **Rootlessness.** No house, no serious relationships, ability to move to a startup hub.
4. **Colleagues.** School concentrates potential [[Cofounders]]. "If your nucleus spits out a neutron, there's a good chance it will hit another nucleus." By 30, people are tied down by jobs and families.
5. **Ignorance.** Paradoxically valuable. "If they'd known how hard it would be, they would have been too intimidated to start." Ignorance also leads to reinvention that can be better than what preceded it.

**Disadvantage of being too young** [3]:

The biggest disadvantage is building things that look like class projects -- solving fake problems with implementation-heavy solutions, not understanding that "the market doesn't give a shit how hard you worked." Class projects are measured by distance from starting point. Real startups are measured by distance remaining to what users need.

The critical thing 24-year-olds have that 20-year-olds lack is understanding the relationship between money and work [3]. "To the average undergrad the idea of getting rich translates into buying Ferraris. To someone who has learned from experience about the relationship between money and work, it translates to something way more important: it means you get to opt out of the brutal equation that governs the lives of 99.9% of people."

**Graham's evolved position on college founders:** YC initially encouraged starting in college but now tells students to wait until graduation [2]. The rationale: starting in college provides a built-in escape hatch (you're still "a student"), which weakens commitment. The exception is founders who can't be talked out of it. "Our official policy now is only to fund undergrads we can't talk out of it" [2].

## The Three Career Paths in Tech

[[Michael Seibel]] lays out three distinct paths: founder, executive, and employee [8]. He attaches no value judgments: "In my ten years in The Bay Area I've seen friends lead successful and fulfilled lives following all three" [8].

**Founder prerequisites (in order of importance)** [8]: (1) Potential teammates with technical skills to build an MVP, (2) a financial plan for 6-12 months, (3) a problem you are passionate about solving, (4) an idea for how to solve it. "A popular mistake is trying to hustle *around* a missing prerequisite instead of *solving the underlying issue*." If the team lacks technical skills, "don't work with a dev shop. Make friends with people who do have these skills. Convince them to join you." Experience is "over-valued (not completely unimportant -- but massively over-valued)" [8].

**Executive path** [8]: Two strategies exist. (1) Pick a rapidly growing company early and ride the growth. "If you were one of the first 100 people at Facebook and you stayed there for ten years, you would have many opportunities to become an executive." (2) Move diagonally up between brand-name companies until reaching an executive role.

**Employee path** [8]: Stable income, more hands-on work, flexibility with high-demand skills. Strategy mirrors the executive path -- join a growing company or a well-known one. "It's much easier to go between brand name companies when you start with a brand name company."

Seibel warns against spending a decade in self-discovery: "if it takes 5-10 years to truly get good at something and you spend 10 years discovering what you want to get good at... it's going to take a long time for you to feel like a highly skilled productive person" [8].

## Seibel's Letter to College Students

[[Michael Seibel]] identifies specific decisions made in college that later create barriers to starting a startup [7]: not learning to code, never meeting anyone who can code, increasing expenses early and becoming unable to afford starting a company, and creating roots in a non-startup-friendly city.

Key advice [7]:
- **Learn to code.** "The number one barrier to starting a tech startup is not knowing how to code." Being a programmer is "the proverbial golden ticket to the *Willy Wonka* factory of the technology industry."
- **Meet people with real skills.** "It's very likely that these people could become your [[Cofounders]] or early employees one day."
- **Have fun.** "For most successful founders, college grades and accolades had almost nothing to do with their future success."
- **Take responsibility.** Join leadership roles in clubs executing real-world tasks.
- **Hit the ground running.** "Your 20s are your most valuable working years" -- few obligations, low burn rate, still able to meet new people and learn new skills.
- **Be your own career counselor.** "Career counseling at most universities is really really bad."
- **Ignore your parents if needed.** "The number of doctors and lawyers I know who really don't love their jobs or their lives is astounding."
- **Be careful with student debt.** "Student debt is forcing people away from startups and into big companies."

Seibel's urgency: "if you are between the ages of 22 and 32 you are in your best startup years. Every year you are not doing a startup you are losing 10% of this prime time" [7].

## From Hackathon to Company: Athelas

[[Tanay Tandon]] describes how [[Athelas]] (YC S16) evolved from a proof-of-concept at YC Hacks 2014 into a company shipping $250 medical devices [9]. The first prototype: a rubber piece and spherical magnifier on a smartphone camera, with a toilet paper roll holding the blood sample, running computer vision for malaria cell counts. During YC (full-time), the team validated white blood cell counts across 350 patients against gold-standard Beckman Counters and submitted to the FDA for 510k approval [9].

## Finding Something Worth Building: Blake Scholl

[[Blake Scholl]] ([[Boom Supersonic]], YC W16) describes making a list of "startup ideas in descending order of how awesome it would be if they worked and completely leaving aside everything else" [10]. His advice: "Find something in the world that you desperately want to exist." The counterintuitive insight: "the bigger the idea actually, the easier it can be because it motivates you. It motivates the people around you. You can attract better people" [10]. But: "don't start a startup just to start a startup. I did that my first time and it was a horrible idea" [10].

## The Half-Hearted Effort Problem

[[Paul Graham]] observes in [[Startup Mistakes]] that the most common failure is not spectacular flameouts but projects started on the side that never go anywhere [12]. "Most founders of failed startups don't quit their day jobs, and most founders of successful ones do." The number who could have succeeded with full commitment is "probably an order of magnitude larger than the number who do make it" [12].

## Working at a Startup: Practical Advice

[[Ryan Choi]] (YC) advises job seekers considering startups to ask about **runway** and **burn rate** [11]. Many YC startups are well-funded, "some even with $10M+ raised." Founders should be transparent about these numbers. See also [[Financial Survival]].

For resumes, Choi recommends covering three things for each position: **what** you worked on, **how** you did it (technologies, team size, cross-functional work), and **impact** (growth numbers, cost savings, sales increase) [11]. On cold outreach: "suggest ways you'd be excited to contribute; include these suggestions on your resume, cover letter or in an intro email." At Twitter, Choi hired people who "stood out because they showed initiative: suggesting strong product ideas/improvements, outlining plans for go-to-market, or even writing sample code" [11]. When interviewing at Lyft, he "reversed engineered the API and built a small SDK" -- it took a couple nights with WireShark, but landed him the job [11].

## What to Do in College

Graham's ultimate advice for students: **"Just learn."** [2]

Starting a startup in your early 20s is "like a brutally fast depth-first search. Most people should still be searching breadth-first at 20" [2]. The things you can do in your early 20s -- plunging deeply into projects on a whim, traveling cheaply -- become impossible once a startup consumes your life.

Specific guidance [2][3]:

- **Don't study entrepreneurship.** "The way to become Larry Page was to become an expert on search. And the way to become an expert on search was to be driven by genuine curiosity" [2]. Taking classes on convertible notes or employee agreements is not just unnecessary but a warning sign of "playing house."
- **Learn powerful things.** "If you want to start a startup after college, what you should do in college is learn powerful things" [2]. Follow genuine intellectual curiosity.
- **Build things.** Look for peers who are "incurable builders" -- people who keep starting projects and finish at least some of them [3]. "Above all else, above academic credentials and even the idea you apply with, we look for people who build things."
- **Find potential [[Cofounders]].** Work on projects with classmates [3]. "Many students feel they should wait and get a little more experience before they start a company... Most students don't realize how rich they are in the scarcest ingredient in startups, co-founders."
- **Learn coding.** Even non-technical founders should understand how software works [5].
- **Develop the habit of thinking about users.** "The concept of users is missing from most college programming classes" [3].

The YC guide "Startups for Students" (by Tamanna Khemani and Kat Manalac) reinforces these themes and adds: roughly 90% of startups fail, but over 50% of YC companies older than five years are still alive [5]. Only 7.9% of YC's top companies were founded by solo founders. Only 3 companies on YC's Top Companies list (Flexport, SnapDocs, UpKeep) were founded by a non-technical founder [5].

## Working at a Startup

For those who decide founding isn't right for them, working at an early-stage startup is the next best thing [1]. Graham argues it's "a lot like being a founder" -- roughly "1/n^2 founder, where n is your employee number." The Startups for Students guide recommends [5]:

- Use YC's [Work at a Startup](https://www.workatastartup.com/jobs) to apply to multiple companies with one resume.
- For internships, prefer post-Series-A companies (more structure, mentorship). For self-directed learners, seed-stage companies work.
- Ask founders: What will I work on? Will I see how the business is doing? Will I interact with customers?
- Ask about runway and burn rate -- this reveals both the startup's health and the founders' transparency.

Graham cautions against working at companies that are too comfortable [3]. Google in 2006 was "a black hole" for startup founders: "zero startups come out of there. People seem to like working at Google too much to leave." A pleasant employer can represent a local maximum, requiring activation energy to escape.

## References

1. [Why to Not Not Start a Startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8x-why-to-not-not-start-a-startup) — [[Paul Graham]]
2. [Before the Startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8y-before-the-startup) — [[Paul Graham]]
3. [A Student's Guide to Startups](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/93-a-student-s-guide-to-startups) — [[Paul Graham]]
4. [Why Smart People Have Bad Ideas](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/91-why-smart-people-have-bad-ideas) — [[Paul Graham]]
5. [Startups for Students](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/D0-startups-for-students) — Tamanna Khemani, Kat Manalac
6. [The Hardest Lessons for Startups to Learn](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8w-the-hardest-lessons-for-startups-to-learn) — [[Paul Graham]]
7. [A Letter to College Students](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4d-a-letter-to-college-students) — [[Michael Seibel]]
8. [Three Paths in the Tech Industry: Founder, Executive, or Employee](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4h-three-paths-in-the-tech-industry-founder-executive-or-employee) — [[Michael Seibel]]
9. [On Starting a Company from a Hackathon Project](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4x-on-starting-a-company-from-a-hackathon-project) — [[Tanay Tandon]]
10. [On Starting and Scaling Aerospace Company Boom](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/5Q-on-starting-and-scaling-aerospace-company-boom) — [[Blake Scholl]]
11. [Advice for Job Seekers on Resumes and Reaching Out](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4l-advice-for-job-seekers-on-resumes-and-reaching-out) — Ryan Choi
12. [The 18 Mistakes That Kill Startups](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8t-the-18-mistakes-that-kill-startups) — [[Paul Graham]]

title: Startup Mistakes
body:
# Startup Mistakes

[[Paul Graham]] identifies 18 mistakes that kill startups [1]. The unifying theme: "In a sense there's just one mistake that kills startups: not making something users want. If you make something users want, you'll probably be fine, whatever else you do or don't do" [1]. The 18 mistakes are all ways startups fail to make something users want.

## 1. Single Founder

"Have you ever noticed how few successful startups were founded by just one person? Even companies you think of as having one founder, like Oracle, usually turn out to have more" [1]. See also [[Cofounders]].

A single founder is a vote of no confidence -- it suggests the founder could not convince any friends to join. Friends know the founder best [1]. But even if they are wrong, a solo founder is at a disadvantage: "Starting a startup is too hard for one person. Even if you could do all the work yourself, you need colleagues to brainstorm with, to talk you out of stupid decisions, and to cheer you up when things go wrong" [1].

The last point may be the most important. "The low points in a startup are so low that few could bear them alone. When you have multiple founders, esprit de corps binds them together in a way that seems to violate conservation laws. Each thinks 'I can't let my friends down'" [1].

## 2. Bad Location

"Startups prosper in some places and not others. Silicon Valley dominates, then Boston, then Seattle, Austin, Denver, and New York" [1]. The falloff is sharp -- in Houston, Chicago, and Detroit, the number of startups per capita is "too small to measure" [1].

The same dynamic exists in every industry: "What's the sixth largest fashion center in the US?" [1]. Startups prosper where the experts are, standards are higher, people are sympathetic, hires want to live, and chance meetings yield useful connections [1].

## 3. Marginal Niche

Choosing a small, obscure niche to avoid competition [1]. Graham compares it to an 8-year-old outfielder closing his eyes when a fly ball comes: "if you make anything good, you're going to have competitors, so you may as well face that. You can only avoid competition by avoiding good ideas" [1].

This shrinking from big problems is mostly unconscious. A remedy: "What would be a great idea for *someone else* to do as a startup?" [1]. See also [[Startup Ideas]].

## 4. Derivative Idea

Imitating an existing company is "one source of ideas, but not the best" [1]. Successful startups usually originate from "some specific, unsolved problem the founders identified." Apple happened because Wozniak wanted a computer, Google because Larry and Sergey could not find things online, Hotmail because Bhatia and Smith could not exchange email at work [1].

"Instead of starting from companies and working back to the problems they solved, look for problems and imagine the company that might solve them" [1].

## 5. Obstinacy

"The stick-to-your-vision approach works for something like winning an Olympic gold medal, where the problem is well-defined. Startups are more like science, where you need to follow the trail wherever it leads" [1]. Most successful startups end up doing something different than they originally intended -- "often so different that it doesn't even seem like the same company" [1].

But constant switching is equally fatal. A test: "If in each new idea you're able to re-use most of what you built for the previous ones, then you're probably in a process that converges" [1].

## 6. Hiring Bad Programmers

Not a problem when founders are programmers. But business-guy-led startups of the 1990s e-commerce era were killed by this [1]. "Business guys can't tell which are the good programmers. They don't even get a shot at the best ones, because no one really good wants a job implementing the vision of a business guy" [1].

The result: "their startup lumbers along like a World War II bomber while their competitors scream past like jet fighters" [1]. See also [[Hiring]].

## 7. Choosing the Wrong Platform

Hotmail was still running on FreeBSD years after Microsoft bought it "presumably because Windows couldn't handle the load" [1]. PayPal's Max Levchin showed their software "scaled only 1% as well on Windows as Unix" -- the company nearly died when a new CEO wanted to switch. "Fortunately for PayPal they switched CEOs instead" [1].

How to choose: let good programmers decide, or "visit a top computer science department and see what they use in research projects" [1].

## 8. Slowness in Launching

"Software is always 85% done" [1]. One reason to launch quickly: "Nothing is truly finished till it's released." Another: "it's only by bouncing your idea off users that you fully understand it" [1].

Delays manifest as: working too slowly, not understanding the problem, fear of users, fear of judgment, working on too many things, excessive perfectionism [1]. The remedy: force yourself to launch something fairly quickly. See also [[Do Things That Don't Scale]].

## 9. Launching Too Early

Less common than launching too slowly ("probably killed a hundred times more startups"), but possible [1]. The danger: ruining your reputation with early adopters who never come back [1].

The minimum viable launch: "identify a core that's both (a) useful on its own and (b) something that can be incrementally expanded into the whole project" [1]. Early adopters "don't expect a newly launched product to do everything; it just has to do *something*" [1].

## 10. Having No Specific User in Mind

"You can't build things users like without understanding them" [1]. The most successful startups often started by solving the founders' own problems. "Perhaps you create wealth in proportion to how well you understand the problem you're solving" [1].

If building for others: "You're flying on instruments" -- you must shift gears, stop relying on intuition, and measure user responses empirically [1]. "If you're going to make something for teenagers or 'business' users or some other group that doesn't include you, you have to be able to talk some specific ones into using what you're making" [1].

## 11. Raising Too Little Money

"Startup funding is measured in time" -- runway [1]. "Too little money means not enough to get airborne." You must raise enough to reach the next milestone: if you have an idea, a working prototype; if a prototype, a launch; if launched, significant growth [1]. See also [[Fundraising]] and [[Default Alive or Default Dead]].

Graham advises: "spend practically nothing, and make your initial goal simply to build a solid prototype. This gives you maximum flexibility" [1]. Take 50-100% more than you think you need "because software takes longer to write and deals longer to close than you expect" [1].

## 12. Spending Too Much

"The classic way to burn through cash is by hiring a lot of people. This bites you twice: in addition to increasing your costs, it slows you down" [1]. Three suggestions: "(a) don't do it if you can avoid it, (b) pay people with equity rather than salary, not just to save money, but because you want the kind of people who are committed enough to prefer that, and (c) only hire people who are either going to write code or go out and get users, because those are the only things you need at first" [1].

## 13. Raising Too Much Money

"Once you take several million dollars of my money, the clock is ticking" [1]. Taking too much money changes the company: you move to proper offices, hire employees (not founders), and encounter office politics. "When you raise a lot of money, your company moves to the suburbs and has kids" [1].

More dangerously, it becomes harder to change direction. "The more people you have, the more you stay pointed in the same direction" [1]. See also [[Financial Survival]].

Graham advises: "take the first reasonable deal you get" from a reputable firm at a reasonable valuation. "Who cares if you could get a 30% better deal elsewhere? Economically, startups are an all-or-nothing game" [1].

## 14. Poor Investor Management

"You shouldn't ignore them, because they may have useful insights. But neither should you let them run the company" [1]. If investors had the vision to run companies, "why didn't they start them?" [1]

"Pissing off investors by ignoring them is probably less dangerous than caving in to them" [1]. With VC-scale money, investors get significant control. When things go well, they leave you alone. When they don't, they can cause serious harm -- even Apple's board "made a nearly fatal blunder in firing Steve Jobs" [1].

## 15. Sacrificing Users to (Supposed) Profit

"Making something people want is so much harder" than making money from it [1]. Business models should be left for later, like "some trivial but messy feature for version 2." The core problem is creating wealth (how much people want something times the number who want it), not converting wealth into money [1].

"It *is* irresponsible not to think about business models. It's just ten times more irresponsible not to think about the product" [1].

## 16. Not Wanting to Get Your Hands Dirty

Larry and Sergey initially tried to get someone to buy their search algorithm rather than start a company. "There's not much of a market for ideas. No one trusts an idea till you embody it in a product and use that to grow a user base" [1]. See also [[Do Things That Don't Scale]].

"If you're going to attract users, you'll probably have to get up from your computer and go find some" [1]. In YC's first batch (summer 2005), the startup whose founder spent half his time talking to cell phone executives was "the most successful of that group by an order of magnitude" [1].

## 17. Fights Between Founders

"About 20% of the startups we've funded have had a founder leave" [1]. YC now advises founders to vest so there is an orderly way for people to quit. A founder leaving does not necessarily kill the startup -- "Blogger got down to one person, and they bounced back" [1]. See also [[Cofounders]].

Most disputes "could have been avoided if they'd been more careful about who they started a company with" [1]. Graham advises: "Don't suppress misgivings. It's much easier to fix problems before the company is started than after." Do not include your housemate because he would feel left out, or someone you dislike because they have a skill you need [1].

## 18. A Half-Hearted Effort

"The most common type is not the one that makes spectacular mistakes, but the one that doesn't do much of anything -- the one we never even hear about" [1]. Statistically, most founders of failed startups do not quit their day jobs, and most founders of successful ones do [1].

Graham estimates the number of people who could have succeeded if they had taken the leap is "probably an order of magnitude larger than the number who do make it" [1].

"Starting startups is just like everything else. The biggest mistake you can make is not to try hard enough" [1].

## References

1. [The 18 Mistakes That Kill Startups](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8t-the-18-mistakes-that-kill-startups) — [[Paul Graham]]

title: YC Essential Startup Advice
body:
# YC Essential Startup Advice

This is YC's compilation of its most important, most transformative advice for startups [1]. It synthesizes principles from multiple YC partners into a single canonical reference.

## Launch Immediately

"The first thing we always tell founders is to launch their product right away; for the simple reason that this is the only way to fully understand customers' problems and whether the product meets their needs" [1]. Launching a mediocre product and iterating is better than waiting for the "perfect" product, as long as the product contains a "quantum of utility" whose value overwhelms any rough edges [1].

## Do Things That Don't Scale

After launch, [[Do Things That Don't Scale|do things that don't scale]] (per [[Paul Graham]]'s essay [Do Things That Don't Scale](http://paulgraham.com/ds.html)) [1]. Many advisors push premature scaling, which wastes time building technology and processes that may not be needed. Instead, get the first customer by any means necessary, "even by manual work that couldn't be managed for more than ten, much less 100 or 1000 customers" [1].

The Airbnb example: founders offered to "professionally" photograph homes of their earliest customers, then went and took the photos themselves [1]. Entirely unscalable, but proved essential for learning how to build a vibrant marketplace.

## The 90/10 Solution

[[Paul Buchheit]] advises looking for a way to accomplish 90% of what you want with only 10% of the work [1]. "If you search hard for it, there is almost always a 90/10 solution available." A 90% solution available right now beats a 100% solution that takes ages to build [1].

## Write Code, Talk to Users

The most important tasks for an early-stage company [1]. All other activities (conferences, dinners, VC meetings, press) are distractions from this core loop. [[Jessica Livingston]] created a list of wrong things to focus on in [How Not To Fail](https://blog.ycombinator.com/how-not-to-fail/) [1]. [[Sam Altman]] calls non-essential activities "fake work" because "it tends to be more fun than real work" [1].

Altman also notes that "it is nearly always better to take the more ambitious path" when choosing between directions [1].

## Choosing Customers

A small group of customers who love you is better than a large group who kind of like you [1]. "Recruiting 10 customers who have a burning problem is much better than 1000 customers who have a passing annoyance" [1].

Sometimes startups need to fire their customers. [[Justin.tv]]/[[Twitch]] only became a breakout success when they focused on video game broadcasters and stopped serving people trying to stream copyrighted content (per [[Michael Seibel]]'s [Users You Don't Want](http://www.michaelseibel.com/blog/users-you-don-t-want)) [1].

## Growth and Product-Market Fit

Growth is the natural result of building something users want, talking to them, and iterating quickly [1]. Growth is not always the right choice: without [[Product-Market Fit|product-market fit]], growth leads to poor retention. With unprofitable unit economics, growth drains cash [1].

[[Paul Buchheit]]: "it never makes sense to take 80 cents from a customer and then hand them a dollar back" [1].

Founders' intuition is always to do more; "usually the best strategy is almost always to do less, really well" [1]. Choose one or two key metrics early, and base decisions nearly exclusively on those metrics [1].

## Deals with Large Companies

"Deals between large companies and tiny startups seldom end well for the startup. They take too long, cost too much, and often fail completely" [1]. Avoid chasing big company validation at the expense of building product.

Also: avoid corporate development conversations. [[Paul Graham]] wrote about this in [Don't Talk to Corp Dev](http://www.paulgraham.com/corpdev.html) [1].

## Competition

"Startup companies always die of suicide not murder" [1]. Spending time worrying about competitors is nearly always a bad idea in the first year or two [1].

## Founder Psychology

Nearly every startup has deep, fundamental issues, even those that become billion-dollar companies [1]. "Your job as a founder will often seem to be continuously righting a capsized ship. This is normal" [1].

Companies most often fail because founders fall out, not because they run out of money [1]. "The relationships with your cofounders matter more than you think and open, honest communications between founders makes future debacles much less likely" [1]. See also [[Cofounders]].

[[Paul Graham]]: "one of the best things you can do to make your startup successful, in fact, to be successful in life, is to simply be nice" [1].

## The Pocket Guide

- Launch now
- Build something people want
- [[Do Things That Don't Scale|Do things that don't scale]]
- Find the 90/10 solution
- Find 10-100 customers who love your product
- All startups are badly broken at some point
- Write code, talk to users
- "It's not your money"
- Growth is the result of a great product not the precursor
- Don't scale your team/product until you have built something people want
- Valuation is not equal to success or even probability of success
- Avoid long negotiated deals with big customers if you can
- Avoid big company corporate development queries
- Avoid conferences unless they are the best way to get customers
- Pre-[[Product-Market Fit|product market fit]]: do things that don't scale, remain small/nimble
- Startups can only solve one problem well at any given time
- Founder relationships matter more than you think
- Sometimes you need to fire your customers
- Ignore your competitors, you will more likely die of suicide than murder
- Most companies don't die because they run out of money
- Be nice! Or at least don't be a jerk
- Get sleep and exercise, take care of yourself

## References

1. [YC's Essential Startup Advice](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4D-yc-s-essential-startup-advice) — Y Combinator ([[Paul Graham]], [[Sam Altman]], [[Michael Seibel]], [[Paul Buchheit]], [[Jessica Livingston]], [[Geoff Ralston]])
