title: Financial Survival
body:
# Financial Survival

Managing cash and runway is a survival skill for startup founders. [[Dalton Caldwell]] addresses the specific situation of having 12 months or less of runway [1], while YC's essential advice covers the broader principles of financial discipline [2]. The foundational concepts come from [[Paul Graham]]'s essays [The Fatal Pinch](http://paulgraham.com/pinch.html) and [[Default Alive or Default Dead]] [3].

The dangers of overhiring and overspending are among the most reinforced principles in the YC library, cited by [[Paul Graham]] [3][4], [[Dalton Caldwell]] [1], [[Sam Altman]] [2], [[Paul Buchheit]] [2], and [[Aaron King]] [5] across 6 sources. The consensus: most startups that die after raising money die because they hired too fast, not because they lacked product ideas.

## Default Alive or Default Dead

The first step is understanding exactly how much cash and runway you have [1][3]. If you are Default Dead (will run out of money at current trajectory), "it is your responsibility as a founder to immediately take actions to become Default Alive" [1]. The mechanisms are straightforward: grow revenue more quickly, cut costs, or both.

## Managing Psychology

[[Dalton Caldwell]] identifies the hardest part of a low-runway situation as "managing your own psychology" [1]. Founders must "simultaneously manage your own anxiety to not be overly negative about your prospects, but also not be irrationally positive." This is a delicate balance. See also [[Founder Mindset]] for more on the psychology of managing through adversity.

## Counter-Productive Ideas

Founders often get caught in thought cycles that cause inaction [1]. Caldwell lists common rationalizations that keep founders Default Dead:

- "The Fatal Pinch does not apply to me"
- "Investors will continue to fund my company if I run out of money"
- "If I fail at fundraising I can just sell the company"
- "My conversations with potential acquirers or investors are very far along and likely to happen"
- "Acquirers won't buy us if I cut costs"
- "My employee morale will plummet if I cut costs"
- "New investors won't fund us if I cut costs"

Caldwell warns: "Don't let these ideas be the justification(s) for why you choose to remain Default Dead" [1].

## Negotiation Leverage Erodes Over Time

A critical insight from Caldwell: delaying action to reduce burn is a bad strategy [1]. From a game theory perspective, "an investor or acquirer is best served to stall and drag you along until you have no leverage at all." An opportunistic party is unlikely to say "no" outright, and will keep their options open as the founder becomes increasingly desperate.

"If things look bleak now and you take no corrective action, it is overwhelmingly likely the situation will get worse, not better" [1].

## Reducing Burn

The least painful path is to make more money immediately [1]. If that is not possible, cut costs.

**Payroll**: The most likely source of high burn. Over-hiring is usually the root cause (per [The Fatal Pinch](http://paulgraham.com/pinch.html)) [1][3]. If reducing staff, treat former employees well and be transparent with remaining employees. "You should always treat your staff as you would want to be treated" [1].

**Real estate**: Leases are binding and very difficult to exit [1]. Real estate obligations are "a common cause of death for later stage companies."

**Easy cuts**: PR expenses, marketing expenses, perks, parties. "Don't blow your money on this stuff" [1].

## The Point of No Return

With less than three months of cash, face the issue head on [1]. "In many cases, less than 2 months is the point of no return."

At this point [1]:
- Lay off employees and give them severance
- Pay down obligations
- Use remaining cash for shutdown costs
- "Do not become insolvent. Pay your debts. You must pay your tax and payroll obligations."
- In messy scenarios, personal liability may arise. Consult lawyers for an orderly shutdown.
- "Don't drag things out and end up in a no upside situation: no upside for you, for your employees, for your investors, or for your customers."

On reputation: "it's just as important to handle situations well when things go poorly as it is when they are going great" [1].

Caldwell's final summary: "1) don't lie to yourself and 2) act quickly and decisively" [1].

## Unit Economics

YC's essential advice emphasizes that growth on unprofitable unit economics merely drains cash [2]. [[Paul Buchheit]] says "it never makes sense to take 80 cents from a customer and then hand them a dollar back" [2]. [[Sam Altman]] wrote about [unit economics](http://blog.samaltman.com/unit-economics) as a concept too many startups forget [2].

## Graham's Default Alive/Dead Framework (In Depth)

[[Paul Graham]]'s essay "[[Default Alive or Default Dead]]" provides the foundational diagnostic [3]. When talking to any startup past 8-9 months, Graham's first question is always: "Assuming their expenses remain constant and their revenue growth is what it has been over the last several months, do they make it to profitability on the money they have left?" Trevor Blackwell built a [calculator](http://growth.tlb.org/#) for this [3].

"The startling thing is how often the founders themselves don't know. Half the founders I talk to don't know whether they're default alive or default dead" [3].

Graham recommends starting to ask this question early -- earlier than feels natural [3]. "It's probably not that dangerous to start worrying too early that you're default dead, whereas it's very dangerous to start worrying too late."

### The Fatal Pinch

The terminal condition: default dead + slow growth + not enough time to fix it [3]. Founders reach this by assuming more money will come. "That assumption is often false, and worse still, the more you depend on it, the falser it becomes."

Graham's advice: separate facts from hopes [3]. Say aloud: "We're default dead, but we're counting on investors to save us." Even with steep growth (5x/year), investors are fickle. [[Fundraising]] should never be more than plan A. "You should always have a plan B: you should know (as in write down) precisely what you'll need to do to survive if you can't raise more money" [3].

### Overhiring: The Top Killer

"Don't hire too fast. Hiring too fast is by far the biggest killer of startups that raise money" [3]. Founders overestimate the need to hire because: (1) there's so much work to do, (2) successful startups have big teams (but this is effect, not cause), and (3) facing slow growth is painful and [[Hiring]] is a distraction from the real problem -- the product isn't appealing enough [3].

VCs often push founders to overhire [3]. "Kill-or-cure strategies are optimal for VCs because they're protected by the portfolio effect. VCs want to blow you up, in one sense of the phrase or the other. But as a founder your incentives are different. You want above all to survive."

The common death pattern: make something moderately appealing, raise a round, hire aggressively to boost growth, growth doesn't come because the product is the problem not headcount, burn through runway, become unappealing to next-round investors, die [3]. The fix: address the product directly. "At this early stage, the product needs to evolve more than to be 'built out,' and that's usually easier with fewer people" [3].

[[Sam Altman]] adds: "I think it's roughly correct to say that YC's most successful companies have never been the fastest to hire, and one of the marks of a great founder is being able to resist this urge" [3].

[[Paul Buchheit]] identifies premature scaling as a related failure mode: "founders take a small business that isn't really working (bad unit economics, typically) and then scale it up because they want impressive growth numbers" [2].

### Growth and Spending Are Surprisingly Unconnected

Graham's empirical observation: "In practice there is surprisingly little connection between how much a startup spends and how fast it grows" [3]. Fast growth comes from hitting a big need. High spending comes from expensive development, expensive sales, or waste. These are different phenomena. Airbnb waited 4 months after raising YC money before hiring their first employee [3]. "The founders were terribly overworked. But they were overworked evolving Airbnb into the astonishingly successful organism it is now."

## Capital Efficiency: The Snapdocs Case

[[Aaron King]] of Snapdocs (YC W14) built a $1.5B company with extreme capital discipline [5].

- Started Snapdocs as a side project years before YC; had product, customers, and revenue when he applied [5].
- Spent ~$1M of the seed round and reached $5M revenue run rate before raising Series A **three years later** [5].
- Lead Series A investor remarked on their capital efficiency vs. peers [5].
- Hired "only when there was a clear and painful need." Made the company harder to run but "incredibly productive" [5].
- Treated early employees like founding team members: generous equity, full transparency about challenges and potential [5].

Capital constraints forced ruthless prioritization: "We couldn't afford to build features that weren't essential" [5]. This kept focus on "going deep with our paying customers." King's insight: "I've learned that you should take the same approach even when you do have the capital to be less disciplined" [5].

When COVID boosted housing demand, Snapdocs shifted to "effectiveness over efficiency," hiring seasoned executives and investing in scale [5]. When demand cooled later: "We ditched the motto of being effective over being efficient. This meant a return to ruthless prioritization" [5]. King's principle: "Startups should scale up at times and scale back at others. It's hard and painful... But these ups and downs are natural and a necessary part of building an enduring company" [5].

## Poverty as Strategic Advantage

In "A Student's Guide to Startups," Graham argues poverty is a genuine advantage [6]. "Recent grads can live on practically nothing, and this gives you an edge over older founders, because the main cost in software startups is people."

More important than living cheaply is **thinking** cheaply [6]. The Apple II was cheap because Woz designed it for himself and couldn't afford more. Viaweb's $300/month pricing was an order of magnitude below competitors. "Like Apple, we created something inexpensive, and therefore popular, simply because we were poor" [6].

The pattern generalizes: "Someone comes along and makes something for a tenth or a hundredth of what it used to cost, and the existing players can't follow because they don't even want to think about a world in which that's possible" [6].

## References

1. [Advice for Companies with Less Than 1 Year of Runway](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/3Z-advice-for-companies-with-less-than-1-year-of-runway) — [[Dalton Caldwell]]
2. [YC's Essential Startup Advice](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4D-yc-s-essential-startup-advice) — Y Combinator
3. [Default Alive or Default Dead](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/95-default-alive-or-default-dead) — [[Paul Graham]]
4. [The Hardest Lessons for Startups to Learn](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8w-the-hardest-lessons-for-startups-to-learn) — [[Paul Graham]]
5. [Snapdocs' Aaron King on Navigating Market Cycles](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Gu-snapdocs-aaron-king-on-navigating-market-cycles) — [[Aaron King]]
6. [A Student's Guide to Startups](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/93-a-student-s-guide-to-startups) — [[Paul Graham]]

title: Product Development
body:
# Product Development

Product development in startups requires structured iteration, clear measurement, and disciplined prioritization. The advice converges on a common theme: build small, measure everything, and let user behavior guide decisions.

## The Development Cycle

[[Michael Seibel]] describes a product development process he refined at Socialcam after years of dysfunction at [[Justin.tv]] [1]. Before developing this process, the team suffered from several problems: meandering product meetings without written decisions, poorly specced products where team members "often had slightly different ideas about what we were building," always wanting to build fully-formed products instead of MVPs, rarely speccing analytics, and development cycles that ran months [1].

### Setting the Cycle Length

The development cycle should be dictated by the product. At Socialcam, building for iOS, they settled on a **two-week cycle** that allowed thorough testing before App Store releases [1]. Web apps can go shorter. Hardware may need longer. The key constraint: "structure the cycle so that teammates stay excited and still feel like they can brainstorm new ideas" [1].

### The Product Meeting

Seibel ran one and only one team meeting: the product meeting on the first day of each dev cycle. "Sometimes this meeting would go for five hours" [1].

Every meeting focused on one of three goals [1]:
1. Increasing content creation
2. Increasing new users
3. Increasing retention

Whichever goal was chosen became the focus for the next two weeks [1].

### Organized Brainstorming

Ideas were written on a whiteboard in three categories: **new features/feature iterations**, **maintenance**, and **A/B tests** [1]. Everyone contributed. "Debates or putting down other people's ideas wasn't permitted. This was the time when everyone felt free to contribute without fear of judgement" [1].

Each item was then graded by the most relevant engineer as [1]:
- **Easy**: Several can be done in a day
- **Medium**: Half a day for one person
- **Hard**: Most of the dev cycle

No item could be so hard it would spill into another cycle -- if it was, it got broken into smaller chunks [1]. This grading had a secondary benefit: "This really helped non-technical people understand which of their ideas were easy to build and which were hard. With this realization they often got better at thinking up easier and easier MVPs of their ideas" [1].

### Building Consensus and Speccing

The team picked items by consensus, starting with hard items (easy to agree on since only one could be done), then medium, then easy [1]. Each item got a detailed spec, an assigned owner, and defined analytics to track. "We would never release a feature without releasing the analytics for that feature and understanding what specific measurable result we wanted to create" [1].

Features were separated into need-to-haves and nice-to-haves. After the meeting, they photographed the whiteboard and deleted it. "We didn't have a product roadmap outside of these two weeks and every product meeting we would start from scratch" [1].

### During the Cycle

After the first Monday, work was quiet. Seibel focused on business/operations tasks, then dug through Mixpanel for product insights or bugs, and ran monthly user testing sessions [1]. Engineers and designers worked on their well-specced projects. During the last three days, all building stopped for testing: "We had a testing list in Excel that included manual tests for all of our basic functionality. Every cycle we added tests for new features built in that cycle and we tested all items on our testing list twice. Everyone on the team tested and we often had competitions for who could test the fastest and who found the most bugs" [1].

### Results at Socialcam

The process enabled rapid iteration: video filters, video borders, video titles, video soundtracks, feed optimizations, multiple visual redesigns, user profiles, recommended channels, front-back camera switching, and more [1]. Growth experiments produced 16 million downloads in about 3 months and over 100 million people watching video on the website in the same period [1]. Seibel notes: "Most importantly though, we did all of this work quickly, efficiently, without major arguments, issues with founder commitment, or really any team problems at all" [1].

## Feature Prioritization Frameworks

[[Emmett Shear]], drawing from his experience building [[Justin.tv]] and [[Twitch]], identifies three mental frameworks for feature prioritization [2]. Startups typically progress through them in order as their challenges shift. (See [[Feature Prioritization Frameworks]] for the full framework.)

### 1. Built For Me

The founder is the primary exemplar user of their own product. "Deeply introspect and figure out what you wish it did instead. Build that" [2].

When it works best [2]:
- When you truly love your product. Shear managed the viewer side of Twitch this way "for a long time, because I really do love watching Twitch."
- For small teams. "It's hard to communicate intuitive introspective understanding to other people."
- When you have good intuitive access to your desires. Shear warns: "I think I want this because it's what I believe I'm supposed to want" feels subjectively identical to "I want this" because human introspection is unreliable.

This framework explains why many of the biggest startups started with the Built For Me approach: "It gives you an automatic advantage against big incumbents who can't use the same method" [2]. Big companies can only use this when the CEO is the lead designer, like Steve Jobs at Apple [2].

### 2. Switch To Us

People are already doing the behavior you want elsewhere. The goal is to get them to do it with you instead [2].

Process [2]:
- Interview 5-6 dedicated users each from your service, each competing service, and closely-adjacent services.
- Categorize and score responses in a spreadsheet.
- Look for patterns. Consider "doubling down on your strengths, and shoring up obvious weaknesses."
- Target one competitor at a time.

The critical insight: "The key to running effective interviews is to use the interviews to understand the problem, and based on what you learn to generate ideas for solving it. Validating an idea you already have is an anti-goal" [2]. You cannot improve an idea through validation. The goal is to have better ideas [2].

When it works best [2]:
- When your product is very important to customers. "Streamers on Twitch have strong opinions about what makes a good streaming service that they can articulate very clearly."
- When you are selling "a better mousetrap, rather than inventing the mousetrap category."

Shear provides specific interview questions for this framework (see [[Feature Prioritization Frameworks]]) [2].

### 3. Three Numbers Matter

Identify the three most important qualities of the product for your customer. All feature decisions flow from these [2].

- **Amazon**: Lower prices, more selection, faster/more reliable shipping.
- **Twitch**: Audience size, positive interactions, money.

"Three is a magic number for focus. Empirically, three seems to be the maximum number of focus areas that people can keep in mind consistently" [2]. A bonus fourth area -- "Stuff You Have To Do" (PCI compliance, uptime) -- is necessary but should not be in the top three [2].

The approach is recursive: each of the three top-level metrics can be decomposed into three sub-drivers. Split investment by intuition -- "maybe it's 33/33/33, maybe it's 50/25/25" [2].

When it works best [2]:
- When people choose your product for measurable reasons (price, audience size, speed).
- Less effective when people choose for hard-to-measure reasons (fun, connection, community).

Shear notes that Twitch still periodically returns to Switch To Us for new customer segments: "as we started working to launch our new music category, we started talking to artists who livestreamed elsewhere online" [2].

## References

1. [Guide to Product Development](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4e-guide-to-product-development) — [[Michael Seibel]]
2. [How to Prioritize Features](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8p-how-to-prioritize-features) — [[Emmett Shear]]
3. [Before Growing Your Startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4p-before-growing-your-startup) — [[Sam Altman]]

title: Growth
body:
# Growth

Growth is the defining characteristic of a startup. [[Paul Graham]] argues that a startup is not merely a newly founded company or one that works on technology -- it is "a company designed to grow fast" [1]. Everything else associated with startups -- venture funding, acquisitions, the high failure rate -- follows from growth.

The primacy of [[Product-Market Fit]] before growth is one of the most reinforced principles in the YC library, cited by [[Paul Graham]] [1], [[Sam Altman]] [2], [[Anu Hariharan]], and [[Gustaf Alstromer]] [3] across 4 sources. The consensus: growth without a product people love is a leaky bucket that burns cash.

## Growth as the Defining Metric

Graham draws a sharp distinction between startups and ordinary businesses [1]. A barbershop is not a startup because it cannot scale: it serves customers in person, and few will travel far for a haircut. A startup must (a) make something lots of people want, and (b) reach and serve all those people. Most businesses are tightly constrained in one of these two dimensions. "The distinctive feature of successful startups is that they're not" [1].

Software is a particularly good way to solve (b), but you can still be constrained in (a) [1]. Graham uses the example: "If you write software to teach Tibetan to Hungarian speakers, you'll be able to reach most of the people who want it, but there won't be many of them. If you make software to teach English to Chinese speakers, however, you're in startup territory."

The growth of a successful startup typically follows three phases, producing an S-curve [1]:

1. An initial period of slow or no growth while the startup figures out what it is doing.
2. A period of rapid growth as the startup discovers how to make something people want and how to reach them.
3. A slowdown as the company becomes large and bumps against the limits of its markets.

The second phase -- the ascent -- defines the startup. Its length and slope determine how big the company will be [1].

## Growth Rates and Compounding

Graham states: "If there's one number every founder should always know, it's the company's growth rate. That's the measure of a startup. If you don't know that number, you don't even know if you're doing well or badly" [1].

He emphasizes that what matters is the ratio of new customers to existing ones, not the absolute number [1]. "If you're really getting a constant number of new customers every month, you're in trouble, because that means your growth rate is decreasing."

During Y Combinator, growth is measured per week [1]. Graham provides specific benchmarks:

- **5-7% per week**: A good growth rate during YC.
- **10% per week**: Exceptionally well.
- **1% per week**: A sign you have not figured out what you are doing.

The best metric to measure is revenue [1]. The next best, for startups not yet charging, is active users, which Graham calls "a reasonable proxy for revenue growth because whenever the startup does start trying to make money, their revenues will probably be a constant multiple of active users" [1].

Small variations in growth rate produce qualitatively different outcomes over time [1]: "A company that grows at 1% a week will grow 1.7x a year, whereas a company that grows at 5% a week will grow 12.6x." A company making $1,000/month growing at 1%/week will be making $7,900/month in four years. At 5%/week, it will be making $25 million/month in four years. Graham observes: "Our ancestors must rarely have encountered cases of exponential growth, because our intuitions are no guide here" [1].

## Growth as Compass

Graham argues that founders should pick a target growth rate and then use it to make every decision: "anything that gets you the growth you need is ipso facto right" [1]. Should you spend two days at a conference? Hire another programmer? Focus on marketing? Court a big customer? Add a feature? "Whatever gets you your target growth rate."

This turns starting a startup into an optimization problem [1]. Graham compares it to optimizing code: "You don't have to think about what the program should do, just make it faster." For founders, the narrow focus creates a similar clarity.

The approach also helps discover better [[Startup Ideas]] [1]. "If you start out with some initial plan and modify it as necessary to keep hitting, say, 10% weekly growth, you may end up with a quite different company than you meant to start. But anything that grows consistently at 10% a week is almost certainly a better idea than you started with."

Graham cautions that within YC, "ipso facto right" excludes "trickery like buying users for more than their lifetime value, counting users as active when they're really not, bleeding out invites at a regularly increasing rate to manufacture a perfect growth curve" [1].

## Don't Grow Before Product-Market Fit

[[Sam Altman]] argues that growth is not step one: "If you focus on trying to grow before you make a product people love, you are unlikely to succeed" [2]. Focusing too heavily on growth too early leads to the "leaky bucket problem" -- users come in the door but do not stay and will not return.

Altman points to specific examples [2]. Facebook had a product people "talked about obsessively from the start." Airbnb "slogged for 1000 days before discovering how to make their product loved. Once they had that, it grew exponentially by word of mouth."

When a product is loved, users become "a free marketing and sales force for you" [2]. The chances of building a large company are "much higher when you have a product that spreads by word of mouth." This applies to enterprise and hard tech companies too -- "your sales process becomes much easier when word has spread that what you're making is great" [2].

Altman warns against papering over a mediocre product with capital: "Don't try to avoid the problem by raising capital for growth -- the problems will still be there, with higher expectations" [2]. He recommends using [[Net Promoter Score]] as one measure of user love. See also [[Early-Stage Tactics]] for more on building before scaling.

If a company is already growing around a mediocre product, Altman advises fixing it immediately [2]. The larger a company grows, "the harder it becomes to course correct. Once your company is a big moving battleship, it becomes really hard to be nimble."

## The Growth Ecosystem: VCs, Founders, and Acquirers

Graham explains why VCs are interested specifically in high-growth companies, not just high-return-to-risk businesses [1]. VCs make returns through capital gains (IPOs or acquisitions), not dividends. Private companies can too easily funnel revenues to insiders while showing little profit, making dividend-based investing impractical without close oversight. With growth-stage startups, "The founders can't enrich themselves without also enriching the investors" [1].

Founders take VC money because growth creates competitive pressure: "If you have such an idea and don't grow fast enough, competitors will. Growing too slowly is particularly dangerous in a business with network effects" [1]. The most successful startups view [[Fundraising]] as buying insurance and choosing their growth rate: "Money to grow faster is always at the command of the most successful startups, because the VCs need them more than they need the VCs" [1].

Acquirers want startups because a rapidly growing company "is not merely valuable, but dangerous" [1]. Most product acquisitions have a component of fear -- the acquirer worries about what a competitor could do with the startup. Graham cites eBay's purchase of PayPal: "Paypal is now responsible for 43% of their sales and probably more of their growth" [1].

## References

1. [Startup = Growth](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8s-startup-growth) — [[Paul Graham]]
2. [Before Growing Your Startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4p-before-growing-your-startup) — [[Sam Altman]]
3. [How to Set Up, Hire, and Scale a Growth Strategy and Team](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/59-how-to-set-up-hire-and-scale-a-growth-strategy-and-team) — [[Anu Hariharan]], Gustaf Alstromer
4. [Later Stage Advice for Startups](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4q-later-stage-advice-for-startups) — [[Sam Altman]]

title: Press and PR
body:
# Press and PR

Press is a small but real part of the founder's job. The best advice, according to [[Michael Seibel]], came from Mike Arrington and M.G. Siegler at an SV Angel CEO conference: "Treat PR like biz dev" [1].

## The Core Principle: PR as Business Development

Seibel spent over $100,000 on PR agencies and PR people as an early-stage startup before learning this lesson [1]. The shift: develop relationships with reporters as people, get introduced through warm connections instead of cold emails, and create a fair value exchange. "Reporters want to talk to CEOs and co-founders -- not PR people" [1].

## Step 1: Generate News

Most founders pitch "profile pieces" about their company. These stories aren't valuable to reporters because they're not timely. "What makes a valuable story is news" [1].

Four categories of startup news [1]:

1. **Product Launches and Features.** Things coming out today, not yesterday or tomorrow. User-facing features. Seibel's examples from Socialcam: releasing video filters, soundtracks, or user profiles [1].

2. **Product/Sales Milestones.** Hitting [a million users](https://zapier.com/blog/one-million-users/), $100k MRR, or [a million guests per night](https://techcrunch.com/2015/05/27/airbnb-hopes-to-have-almost-a-million-stays-a-night-by-summer/) [1]. Seibel recommends sandbagging: "wait until you're 25% past the milestone to announce -- so that you're that much closer to announcing the next milestone" [1].

3. **Significant BD Deals or Customers.** Landing a team at Airbnb or Instacart, or [partnering with GM](https://www.fastcompany.com/3055059/gm-partners-with-lyft-to-build-a-network-of-self-driving-cars) to build a network of self-driving cars. Announce if you have permission [1].

4. **Fundraising.** "For better or worse, fundraising is conflated with traction/success so it is a topic people want to read about" [1].

## Step 2: Build Direct Press Contacts

"Don't spray and pray. It's stupid" [1].

The best method: warm introductions. Have someone who has been covered by the reporter introduce you. Proper etiquette is to ask for an intro to only one reporter per friend [1]. For YC founders, the YC network is valuable since "the reporter you want to meet has definitely written about someone you know" [1].

The intro email should be readable and reply-able in 30 seconds [1]:
- Describe your news in one sentence
- For an [exclusive](https://www.quora.com/What-is-a-press-exclusive-and-how-does-it-work): say so
- For an [embargo](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/News_embargo): include date and time
- Request a 20-minute call

On the call, have 3-5 reasons why your news and company matter. At the end, offer notes and collateral (screenshots, graphs, logos, pictures) -- this provides "an opportunity to be courteous *and* provide reporters with the language you prefer" [1].

## Step 3: Offer Exclusives

"Almost all startups should be offering their story as an exclusive to one tech reporter at a time" [1]. The target audience -- investors, potential employees, motivated customers, early adopters -- exists in high concentration in a few tech outlets. "Getting blanket coverage across a bunch of tech blogs is often not worth the struggle and can piss people off when embargoes are broken" [1].

## Step 4: Maintain Consistent Cadence

Traditional PR philosophy says to maximize mentions per story by combining all news into one big story infrequently. Seibel disagrees for startups: "you're better off getting one story every month or every other month versus one huge story every year" [1].

Spread news across multiple stories. Maintain a queue of 3-5 pieces of news to announce over the coming 6 months. "It takes the pressure off you if one of those pieces of news doesn't get picked up, and it helps you keep a regular cadence of positive news" [1].

After 6-12 months of consistent cadence and real news, you should be "relatively friendly with two to five reporters" -- they have written about your company enough that they reply to most emails and you have probably met in person [1].

## Expectations and Time Allocation

Expect a 25-50% success rate if you are providing genuine news and doing everything right [1].

"Make sure you aren't dedicating more than 5% of your time to press and don't expect PR to create observable miracles (tons of users, customers, investment offers, etc)" [1]. Most benefits only become obvious weeks or months after a story posts. PR is "like funding basic research in science, you can't predict the advances but they do happen when you invest consistently over time" [1].

## Later-Stage PR

[[Sam Altman]] advises companies to "ignore marketing and PR for a long time" in the beginning [2]. "Press will not make your startup" [2]. But once the product is working, "switch from not caring about it to caring about it a little bit" [2].

Key later-stage principles [2]:
- **Never outsource key messaging** to a PR firm or head of marketing. The founder defines the message.
- **Build journalist relationships directly.** "No journalist wants to talk to a PR firm." Pick 3-4 journalists who like and understand you.
- At some point (Seibel cites Dropbox and Airbnb), press organically pays attention [1]. This is when PR people become valuable as a buffer: "They create a buffer that allows you to be more strategic about what you announce, when, and to whom" [1].

## References

1. [Getting Press for Your Startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4c-getting-press-for-your-startup) — [[Michael Seibel]]
2. [Later-Stage Advice for Startups](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4q-later-stage-advice-for-startups) — [[Sam Altman]]

title: Cofounders
body:
# Cofounders

The cofounder relationship is the foundation of a startup. [[Paul Graham]] calls not having a cofounder "a real problem" and states unequivocally: "All investors, without exception, are more likely to fund you with a cofounder than without" [1]. Only 7.9% of YC's [top companies](https://www.ycombinator.com/topcompanies) were founded by solo founders [6].

The importance of the cofounder relationship is one of the most reinforced principles in the YC library, cited by [[Paul Graham]] across 4 separate essays [1][2][3][7], as well as by [[Catheryn Li]] [4] and the YC team [5][6]. Graham returns to it as one of the three factors YC evaluates when deciding whether to fund a startup [7].

## Why You Need a Cofounder

"A startup is too much for one person to bear." This is Graham's blunt assessment in "Why to Not Not Start a Startup" [1]. The reasons are both practical and emotional:

**Workload.** A startup requires simultaneous excellence in building product, finding users, [[Fundraising]], [[Hiring]], and dozens of other tasks. A solo founder must do all of these while also maintaining the psychological resilience to handle constant setbacks [1].

**Emotional support.** Building a startup is mentally taxing. A cofounder gives you someone to share the emotional weight of the journey, to talk through problems, and to maintain morale during the inevitable low points [1].

**Complementary skills.** One cofounder might be a talented programmer while the other is great at sales. The Startups for Students guide notes that only 3 companies on YC's Top Companies list (Flexport, SnapDocs, UpKeep) were founded by a non-technical founder, underscoring the importance of having technical ability on the founding team [6].

**Accountability and commitment.** Graham argues in "Billionaires Build" that the exploitative type of founder "usually begins with their own cofounders, which is disastrous, since the cofounders' relationship is the foundation of the company" [7]. YC looks for authentic relationships where founders genuinely care about what they're building [7].

## Where to Find Cofounders

### School

The most common and best place to find cofounders is school. Graham explains why in "A Student's Guide to Startups" [2]: "You have a large sample of smart people; you get to compare how they all perform on identical tasks; and everyone's life is pretty fluid." Google, Yahoo, and Microsoft were all founded by people who met in school (Microsoft in high school) [2].

"The number one question people ask us at Y Combinator is: Where can I find a co-founder?" Graham writes [2]. "That's the biggest problem for someone starting a startup at 30. When they were in school they knew a lot of good co-founders, but by 30 they've either lost touch with them or these people are tied down by jobs they don't want to leave."

The Startups for Students guide confirms this with data from YC's top companies: the most common way cofounders meet is through school and work [6]. Graham advises students: "Look at the people around you and ask yourself which you'd like to work with." Specifically, look for "incurable builders" -- people who keep starting projects and finish some of them [2].

### Work

Fewer cofounders meet at work than at school, but Graham offers tactical advice: work somewhere with lots of smart, young people, ideally in a startup hub. Check your employment agreement carefully -- most say ideas you develop while employed belong to the company [2].

### Cofounder Matching Platforms

YC runs a [cofounder matching platform](https://www.ycombinator.com/cofounder-matching) where prospective founders describe themselves and what they're looking for, and the platform shows matching profiles. The article "Does Co-Founder Matching Work?" documents three success stories [4]:

**Sequin (YC S21):** Vrinda Gupta had spent a year as a solo founder after leaving Visa, where she'd launched cards like the Chase Sapphire Reserve. She had raised a pre-seed and built an MVP but needed a technical cofounder. She matched with Mark Thomas, who had ten years of engineering experience at PayPal and six years as CTO of family-oriented startups. "We met the day after we matched, and the day after, and then the day after that." They went through YC and [raised $5.7M](https://www.forbes.com/sites/geristengel/2021/09/15/debit-card-startup-builds-credit-for-women/?sh=6283b60b3c67) [4].

**Kiwi Biosciences (YC S21):** Anjie Liu started the company to solve her own irritable bowel syndrome. She had a founding scientist but wanted a cofounder to commercialize the product. She matched with David Hachuel, who had previously sold a startup in the same space. They used a structured approach, answering all 50 questions from [First Round's co-founder questionnaire](https://proof-assets.s3.amazonaws.com/firstround/50%20Questions%20for%20Co-Founders.pdf) and doing a month-long trial project. They worked remotely for four months and met in person only after getting into YC. They [raised $1.5M](https://techcrunch.com/2021/10/14/kiwi-bio-aims-to-free-irritable-bowel-syndrome-sufferers-from-restrictive-diets/) [4].

Graham himself is skeptical that cofounders found through casual meetings (user groups, conferences) will work: "You need to work with someone to know whether you want them as a cofounder" [2].

## How to Evaluate a Potential Cofounder

YC published "10 Questions to Discuss with a Potential Co-Founder," derived from talking to teams who met on YC's cofounder matching and from studying the most common reasons founding teams break up [5]. The [questionnaire](https://yc.link/10-questions-cf) is designed to be used after you've gotten to know someone, found a shared idea, and are deciding whether to commit.

### The 10 Key Questions

1. **Why do you want to do this startup? What are your personal goals, financial and non-financial?** [5]
2. **What will our roles and titles be? How will we divide responsibilities? Who will be CEO?** (Roles change, but have a plan for the first 6-12 months.) [5]
3. **How will we split up equity?** (Resolve this early. YC provides a [guide](https://yc.link/10-questions-cf-equity). See also [[Equity and Compensation]].) [5]
4. **Where will the company be based? In-person or remote?** [5]
5. **What idea will we work on? If it doesn't work, are you willing to change it?** [5]
6. **What needs to happen for each of us to go full-time?** (e.g., raise $xx, validate with a paying customer, enjoy working together for two months.) [5]
7. **What is your personal financial situation? How long can you work without salary?** [5]
8. **What will our typical working schedule be?** [5]
9. **If we build a team, what's important to you about how we do that?** (In-person vs remote, cultural values, [[Hiring]] approach.) [5]
10. **What will we do if we can't agree on an important decision? What happens if we decide to stop working together?** [5]

The goal is not to have all the answers but to detect strong differences early, before they become crises. The recommended process: each founder copies the document, writes answers independently, then shares and discusses [5].

### Additional Relationship-Building Questions

YC also recommends deeper questions for getting to know each other [5]:
- How would friends describe your strengths and weaknesses?
- What are companies or founders you admire and want to model after?
- What kind of work is so fun it doesn't feel like work?
- When stressed, do you want to talk about it or avoid it? (Psychologist Amy Buechler wrote about how [anxious vs. avoidant attachment styles](https://www.notion.so/Anxious-3-Avoidant-Know-your-Attachment-Style-31964247e3274807a32b44caea38db19) affect cofounder relationships.)
- Are there outside pressures (family, visa, health) your cofounder should know about?

### Before Committing Checklist

YC's recommended steps [5]:
- **Meet in person and do a trial project.**
- **Do reference calls on each other** -- transparently swap suggested references.
- **Have your significant other meet your cofounder** (this person will be enormously important in your life).

### After Committing Checklist

- **Create a system to prioritize tasks.** YC recommends their [product development cycle](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4e-product-development-cycle-fundamentals) as one model [5].
- **Set up a weekly "Founder Sync" 1-1** -- Amy Buechler created this concept. It's a recurring meeting specifically about how things are going with working together, not about the business. Her [detailed guide](https://www.notion.so/The-Founder-Sync-54f855a12de749fa9e74e928ca7fa5a5) on running them helps avoid cofounder breakups [5].

## The Cofounder Relationship as Foundation

Graham emphasizes in "Billionaires Build" that YC evaluates the cofounder relationship as one of the three factors in deciding whether to fund: "How determined are the founders? Are they good at building things? Are they resilient enough to keep going when things go wrong? How strong is their friendship?" [7]

Graham's advice about choosing cofounders parallels his advice about choosing [[Startup Ideas]] -- trust your instincts about people even when they seem impressive: "If you're thinking about getting involved with someone -- as a cofounder, an employee, an investor, or an acquirer -- and you have misgivings about them, trust your gut" [3].

The flip side: "You should only start a startup with someone you like, because a startup will put your friendship through a stress test." Work with people you genuinely like and have known long enough to be sure [3].

## References

1. [Why to Not Not Start a Startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8x-why-to-not-not-start-a-startup) — [[Paul Graham]]
2. [A Student's Guide to Startups](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/93-a-student-s-guide-to-startups) — [[Paul Graham]]
3. [Before the Startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8y-before-the-startup) — [[Paul Graham]]
4. [Does Co-Founder Matching Work?](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Cq-does-co-founder-matching-work) — [[Catheryn Li]]
5. [10 Questions to Discuss with a Potential Co-Founder](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Hj-10-questions-to-discuss-with-a-potential-co-founder) — Y Combinator
6. [Startups for Students](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/D0-startups-for-students) — Tamanna Khemani, Kat Manalac
7. [Billionaires Build](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/9M-billionaires-build) — [[Paul Graham]]

title: Fundraising
body:
# Fundraising

Startup fundraising is the process of raising external capital to fund [[Growth]]. Nearly all startups require outside funding because the capital needed to reach profitability exceeds what founders and their networks can provide. [[Geoff Ralston]] describes fundraising as "brutal" but a path "almost all companies and founders must walk" [1]. The core YC advice on fundraising, per [[YC Essential Startup Advice]], is to "raise money as quickly as possible and then get back to work" [7].

Raising quickly and returning to building is one of the most reinforced principles in the YC library, cited by [[Geoff Ralston]] [1], [[Paul Graham]] [8][9], [[Sam Altman]] [15], and the YC team [7] across 16 sources. The consensus is clear: fundraising is a necessary distraction, not the main event.

## Why Raise Money

Cash provides a competitive advantage in [[Hiring]], PR, marketing, and sales [1]. High-growth companies almost always need to burn capital to sustain growth before reaching profitability. A few startups successfully bootstrap, but they are the exception. As [[Geoff Ralston]] writes, "Without startup funding the vast majority of startups will die" [1].

One critical reminder from YC: "the money you raise IS NOT your money. You have a fiduciary and ethical/moral duty to spend the money only to improve the prospects of your company" [7].

## When to Raise

Founders should raise when they have identified the market opportunity, the customer, and have a product that is being adopted at a rapid rate [1]. [[Geoff Ralston]] sets a bar: "a rate of 10% per week for several weeks is impressive" [1]. For founders who can convince investors without traction, the advice is simply: congratulations. For everyone else: "work on your product and talk to your users" [1].

Valuation is not equal to success or even probability of success [7]. [[Michael Seibel]] has emphasized this, and YC notes that "some of Y Combinator's very best companies raised on tiny initial valuations (Airbnb, Dropbox, Twitch, are all good examples)" [7].

## How Much to Raise

The ideal amount gets a startup to profitability so it never needs to raise again [1]. Failing that, raise enough for 12-18 months of runway to reach the next fundable milestone. [[Geoff Ralston]] provides a rule of thumb: an engineer costs roughly $15K/month all-in, so 5 engineers for 18 months requires about $1.35M [1].

Dilution guidance: 10% is wonderful, up to 20% is typical, and founders should try to avoid more than 25% [1]. Seed valuations tend to range from $2M-$10M, but "the goal is not to achieve the best valuation, nor does a high valuation increase your likelihood of success" [1].

Most first seed rounds cluster around $600K, though these have been increasing. The range is typically $500K to $1.5M [1].

## Financing Instruments

### SAFEs (Simple Agreements for Future Equity)

The safe, created by YC, has almost completely replaced convertible debt for seed rounds [1]. A safe has no interest rate, no maturity date, and no repayment requirement. Negotiable terms are typically just the amount, the cap, and the discount. [[Geoff Ralston]] recommends reading the [safe primer](http://www.ycombinator.com/documents/) by [[Carolynn Levy]] [1].

### Convertible Debt

A loan that converts to equity at the next priced round [1]. Has a principal amount, interest rate (usually minimum 2%), maturity date, and typically a cap or discount. Convertible debt may be called at maturity. Largely replaced by SAFEs in Silicon Valley.

### Equity Rounds

Setting a valuation and selling new shares [1]. More complicated, expensive, and time-consuming than SAFEs or convertible notes. Example math from [[Geoff Ralston]]: raising $1M on a $5M pre-money valuation with 10M shares outstanding means selling shares at $0.50, issuing 2M new shares, for a post-money of $6M and dilution of 16.7% (not 20%, because dilution is calculated on the post-money share count) [1].

## Types of Investors

[[Geoff Ralston]] draws a clear distinction: "The difference between an angel and a VC is that angels are amateurs and VCs are pros. VCs invest other people's money and angels invest their own on their own terms" [1]. Angels decide faster, with more emotional input. VCs require more time, more meetings, and multiple partners in the decision.

The ecosystem also includes micro-VCs / super-angels that target very early stage companies, AngelList Syndicates that let angels pool resources, and platforms like FundersClub that let angels become LPs [1].

The best way to meet investors is via warm introduction [1]. If no introduction is possible, send a "brief, but compelling summary of your business and opportunity."

## The Seed Pitch Deck

[[Aaron Harris]] provides YC's seed deck template [2]. The key principle: "founders should strive for clarity and concision. This is not the right place to write a treatise on your market or world philosophy." The structure:

1. **Title page** - Company name and one-line description (the only slide limited to exactly 1 slide) [2]
2. **Problem** - State the problem clearly with real-world impact [2]
3. **Solution** - What you do, in as few words as possible, with concrete benefits [2]
4. **Traction** - Clear, meaningful numbers (charts, metrics) [2]
5. **Insights** - What makes this work, what your unique insight is [2]
6. **Business model** - How you make money [2]
7. **Market** - Size and growth, how much money you will make [2]
8. **Team** - Why founders are well-suited; "nobody cares about your advisors" [2]
9. **The Ask** - How much money you need and what it gets you; if it makes you Series A ready in a year, that is powerful [2]

Each section can use 1-3 slides, but try to keep it to 1. Harris emphasizes: "Focus on narrative. The rest is commentary" [2].

For deck design principles, see [[Pitch Deck Design]], based on [[Kevin Hale]]'s framework of legible, simple, and obvious [3].

## Meeting Investors

From [[Geoff Ralston]]'s advice on investor meetings [1]:

- Know your audience: research what they invest in and why
- Simplify your pitch to the essential: great product, right team, dream of a gigantic company
- "If you can get the investor to talk more than you, your probability of a deal skyrockets"
- Strike a balance between confidence and humility. "Never cross over into arrogance, avoid defensiveness, but also don't be a pushover."
- Never leave a meeting without an attempted close or absolute clarity on next steps

### What Not to Do

Ralston provides a detailed list of don'ts when communicating with investors [1]:
- Don't be dishonest, arrogant, or overly aggressive
- Don't talk so much they can't get a word in
- Don't create detailed financials at seed stage
- Don't use ridiculous market size numbers without justification
- Don't ask for an NDA
- Don't try to negotiate in real-time
- Don't over-optimize your valuation
- Don't take a "No" personally

[[Paul Graham]] captures the psychological reality of rejection: "If the soda is empty, stop making that awful sucking sound with the straw" [8].

## Negotiating and Closing

Deals have momentum [1]. Getting the first investor in is the hardest part; each subsequent close gets faster and easier. [[Geoff Ralston]] advises a "breadth-first search weighted by expected value" approach: talk to as many investors as possible, prioritizing those most likely to close [1].

Once an investor says yes, use a [handshake deal protocol](https://www.ycombinator.com/handshake/) to close rapidly [1]. Get signatures and cash as soon as possible. One reason SAFEs are popular is that closing can take "no longer than a few minutes" via platforms like [Clerky](https://www.clerky.com/) or [Ironclad](https://ironclad.ai/) [1].

## Series A

### The Term Sheet

[[Jason Kwon]] and [[Aaron Harris]] published a standard, clean Series A term sheet through YC's Series A program [4]. The most important insight: "founders don't know what 'good' looks like in a term sheet" because it is often literally the first time they have seen one [4]. See also [[Series A Term Sheet]] for the full framework.

Key points about a standard Series A [4]:
- The lead investor generally wants 20% of the company, but pricing flexes based on leverage
- A founder-friendly board structure is 2-1 (2 founders, 1 investor). Founders most often lose control with a 2-2-1 structure (2 founders, 2 investors, 1 independent), which means "the founders can be fired from their own company." See also [[Board Governance]].
- The term sheet fits on a single page in modern practice

**Dirty terms to watch for** (absent from a clean term sheet) [4]:
- Liquidation preference greater than 1x
- Participating preferred (double-dipping)
- Cumulative dividends (compounding liquidation preference yearly)
- Warrant coverage (extra ownership without paying agreed valuation)

These terms signal that the investor is "sort of afraid of losing my money" [4]. As Kwon and Harris write: "when an investor says that they're committed to partnering with you for the long-term - or that they're betting everything on you - but then tells you something else with the terms that they insist on, believe the terms" [4].

Good investors address economic risk by negotiating valuation, not by adding structure [4].

The Series A documents set a foundation and precedent for future rounds. "Unwinding bad terms is difficult, and oftentimes impossible." But: "No one ever built an enduring company just by winning their Series A negotiation" [4].

### Series A Diligence

[[Jason Kwon]] and [[Aaron Harris]] also published a Series A diligence checklist [5]. Having all documents ready in a data room before signing a term sheet "will cut as much as a week off of your closing process" [5]. Founders often do not realize closing can take more than a month, much of it spent tracking down documents. See also [[Series A Diligence Checklist]] for the full list.

The checklist covers [5]:
- **Corporate records**: minutes, certificates of incorporation, bylaws, org charts
- **Business plan and financials**: current plan, projections, recent financial statements
- **Intellectual property**: trademarks, patents, copyrights, domain names, assignments
- **Security issuances**: stockholder list, option holders, vesting schedules, voting agreements
- **Material agreements**: terms of service, contracts >$25K, leases, debt, insurance, IP licenses
- **Litigation**: pending or threatened actions, IP infringement allegations
- **Employee information**: employee list with titles/salaries/classifications, offer letters, benefit plans, equity grants, handbook

**Critical note on equity grants**: Complete all pending or promised equity grants before starting the Series A process [5]. Once a term sheet is received, many believe the current 409A valuation can no longer be used. Employees hired earlier would then receive equity at a higher strike price, reducing their upside and potentially creating morale issues. See also [[Equity and Compensation]].

### Series A Pitch and Deck

[[Janelle Tam]] provides a complete guide to building a Series A pitch and deck [13]. The Series A deck typically runs 10-15 slides and tells a traction story rather than just a vision story. See [[Series A Pitch and Deck]] for the full framework.

## Fundraising Rules Summary

From [[Geoff Ralston]] [1]:
- Get fundraising over as soon as possible, and get back to building
- But don't stop too soon if it is difficult: keep fighting and stay alive
- Be "greedy": breadth-first search weighted by expected value
- Once someone says yes, get docs signed and money in the bank immediately
- Always hustle for leads
- Never screw anyone over. "The Valley is a very small place, and a bad reputation is difficult to repair."
- Develop a style that fits you and your company
- Stay organized; co-founders should split tasks

## Convincing Investors: Graham's Framework

[[Paul Graham]] frames the fundraising challenge differently from the mechanics-focused approach above [8]. In "How to Convince Investors," he argues that the core problem is not pitch technique but truthful self-evaluation.

"When people hurt themselves lifting heavy things, it's usually because they try to lift with their back. The right way to lift heavy things is to let your legs do the work. Inexperienced founders make the same mistake when trying to convince investors. They try to convince with their pitch. Most would be better off if they let their startup do the work" [8].

### The Three Requirements (Graham)

Investors need three things [8]:

1. **Formidable founders.** "Most investors decide in the first few minutes whether you seem like a winner or a loser." What formidable means: "A formidable person is one who seems like they'll get what they want, regardless of whatever obstacles are in the way. Formidable is roughly justifiably confident." Winners get questions like "yes, and you could also do x." Losers get "but what about y?" Don't fake swagger: "If you try to act like something you're not, you'll just end up in an uncanny valley." See also [[Founder Mindset]].

2. **A promising market.** Investors think of startups as markets. "If there are x customers who'd pay an average of $y per year, the TAM is $xy" [8]. The market doesn't have to be big yet, but there must be "some plausible sequence of hops that leads to dominating a big market." The "why now?" question is critical: ideally something recently changed and no one else has noticed.

3. **Evidence of success.** At Demo Day (3 months), a growth graph is "mostly evidence that the founders are effective" [8]. At Series A (2 years), "it has to be evidence of a promising market and a company tuned to exploit it."

### Truth as Strategy

Graham's core advice: "Convince yourself that your startup is worth investing in, and then when you explain this to investors they'll believe you" [8]. This requires being a domain expert. "If you're not a domain expert, you can be as convinced as you like about your idea, and it will seem to investors no more than an instance of the Dunning-Kruger effect."

School trains everyone to present regardless of substance [8]. Founders approach fundraising the same way, spreading thin truth across a fixed presentation. Graham's corrective: "The time to raise money is not when you need it, or when you reach some artificial deadline like a Demo Day. It's when you can convince investors, and not before."

The recipe for inexperienced founders [8]: (1) Make something worth investing in. (2) Understand why it's worth investing in. (3) Explain that clearly. "If you're saying something you know is true, you'll seem confident when you're saying it."

[[Alfred Lin]] notes that startup explanations must be clear and concise because they convince at one remove: "it has to work not just on the partner you talk to, but when that partner re-tells it to colleagues" [8].

### Handling Rejection

Getting rejected is common at all levels [8]. "A lot of VCs would have rejected Microsoft. Certainly some rejected Google." All East Coast VCs passed on Dropbox; weeks later it raised a Series A from Sequoia. The best response to rejection: talk candidly about what scared investors. The best investors "already believe most other investors are conventional-minded drones" [8].

### Don't Get Your Hopes Up

From "The Hardest Lessons," Graham's deal psychology advice: when someone says "we want to invest," assume they won't [9]. "The reason I warn startups not to get their hopes up is not to save them from being disappointed. It's to prevent them from leaning their company against something that's going to fall over." VCs and corp dev are professional negotiators who exploit perceived weakness. The only real leverage: genuinely not needing the deal [9].

### The YC Interview

In "Billionaires Build," Graham explains YC interviews are explicitly not pitches [10]. Ten-minute conversations requiring random access, not sequential presentations. "It is so annoying when people try to [turn it into a pitch]." The best preparation: talk to your users. The most important single question: **"How do you know people want this?"** [10]

### The Equity Equation for Investment Decisions

Graham's [[The Equity Equation]] provides a mathematical framework: give up n% of the company only if the trade makes the remaining (100-n)% worth more than the whole was before [8]. The formula: 1/(1-n). For YC's 7%, that means 1/.93 = 1.075 -- so YC needs to improve outcomes by more than 7.5% for the deal to be worthwhile. For Sequoia at 30%, 1/.7 = 1.43, meaning a 43% improvement is needed. Graham argues that for most startups, "it would improve the average startup's prospects by more than 43% just to be able to *say* they were funded by Sequoia" [8]. The catch: Sequoia receives ~6,000 plans/year and funds ~20.

### The Valuation Trap

[[Sam Altman]] warns against raising at too high a valuation: "Don't raise at too high a valuation when you have an airplane, not a spaceship" [15]. A high valuation creates expectations the company may not be able to meet, making the next round harder or triggering a damaging down round.

### Common Fundraising Mistakes

[[Paul Graham]] identifies several fundraising-related mistakes that kill startups [14]: raising too little money, spending too much, raising too much (creating unrealistic expectations), and poor investor management. See [[Startup Mistakes]] for the full list.

### Don't Assume More Money Is Coming

From [[Default Alive or Default Dead]], Graham warns: "Don't assume it will be easy to raise more; the more you depend on it, the falser it becomes" [12]. See [[Financial Survival]] for the full framework on managing runway.

## References

1. [A Guide to Seed Fundraising](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4A-a-guide-to-seed-fundraising) — [[Geoff Ralston]]
2. [How to Build Your Seed Round Pitch Deck](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/2u-how-to-build-your-seed-round-pitch-deck) — [[Aaron Harris]]
3. [How to Design a Better Pitch Deck](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4T-how-to-design-a-better-pitch-deck) — [[Kevin Hale]]
4. [A Standard and Clean Series A Term Sheet](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4P-a-standard-and-clean-series-a-term-sheet) — [[Jason Kwon]], [[Aaron Harris]]
5. [Series A Diligence Checklist](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/3h-series-a-diligence-checklist) — [[Aaron Harris]], [[Jason Kwon]]
6. [How Biotech Startup Funding Will Change in the Next 10 Years](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4L-how-biotech-startup-funding-will-change-in-the-next-10-years) — [[Jared Friedman]]
7. [YC's Essential Startup Advice](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4D-yc-s-essential-startup-advice) — Y Combinator
8. [How to Convince Investors](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/98-how-to-convince-investors) — [[Paul Graham]]
9. [The Hardest Lessons for Startups to Learn](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8w-the-hardest-lessons-for-startups-to-learn) — [[Paul Graham]]
10. [Billionaires Build](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/9M-billionaires-build) — [[Paul Graham]]
11. [Before the Startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8y-before-the-startup) — [[Paul Graham]]
12. [Default Alive or Default Dead](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/95-default-alive-or-default-dead) — [[Paul Graham]]
13. [How to Build a Great Series A Pitch and Deck](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8d-how-to-build-a-great-series-a-pitch-and-deck) — [[Janelle Tam]]
14. [The 18 Mistakes That Kill Startups](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8t-the-18-mistakes-that-kill-startups) — [[Paul Graham]]
15. [Navigating Success for Your Startup](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/4s-navigating-success-for-your-startup) — [[Sam Altman]]
16. [Startup = Growth](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8s-startup-growth) — [[Paul Graham]]

title: Early-Stage Tactics
body:
# Early-Stage Tactics

"Actually startups take off because the founders make them take off." This is [[Paul Graham]]'s central argument in "[[Do Things That Don't Scale]]" [1]. The most common misconception among founders is that startups either take off by themselves or don't. In reality, nearly every successful startup required deliberate, laborious, unscalable effort to get started -- "like the cranks that car engines had before they got electric starters" [1].

## Recruit Users Manually

The most common unscalable thing founders must do is recruit users one at a time [1]. This is true even for startups solving urgent problems. Stripe is one of YC's most successful companies, solving a problem every programmer felt acutely. "If anyone could have sat back and waited for users, it was Stripe. But in fact they're famous within YC for aggressive early user acquisition" [1].

The **Collison installation** -- named after Stripe's founders [[Patrick Collison]] and John Collison -- is YC's term for their technique. While diffident founders ask "Will you try our beta?" and send a link, "the Collison brothers weren't going to wait. When anyone agreed to try Stripe they'd say 'Right then, give me your laptop' and set them up on the spot" [1].

Two forces prevent founders from doing this [1]:

1. **Shyness and laziness.** "They'd rather sit at home writing code than go out and talk to a bunch of strangers and probably be rejected by most of them." But at least one founder (usually the CEO) must spend substantial time on sales and marketing.

2. **The absolute numbers seem pathetically small.** "This can't be how the big, famous startups got started, they think." The mistake is underestimating compound growth. 100 users growing at 10% per week becomes 14,000 after a year and 2 million after two years.

**Airbnb** is the canonical example. The founders went door to door in New York, recruiting hosts and helping them improve listings. Graham recalls them always arriving at Tuesday YC dinners with rolly bags, having just flown back from somewhere. "Early on it was so fragile that about 30 days of going out and engaging in person with users made the difference between success and failure" [1].

**Finding your initial users:** If you built something for yourself, finding peers is straightforward. Otherwise, do a comparatively untargeted launch, observe which users seem most enthusiastic, and seek out more like them [1]. Ben Silbermann noticed early Pinterest users were interested in design, so he went to a conference of design bloggers -- and that worked well [1].

## Delight Your Users

"You should take extraordinary measures not just to acquire users, but also to make them happy" [1]. Wufoo sent each new user a hand-written thank you note, and maintained this surprisingly long.

Three reasons founders fail to do this [1]:

1. **Engineering training.** Engineers learn to build robust and elegant systems, not to be "slavishly attentive to individual users." But as Graham notes, early startup founders aren't Scotty (the engineer in the back); they're Kirk (running the whole show).

2. **Worry about scale.** Founders of tiny startups worry that over-serving users won't scale. Graham's response: "In their current state they have nothing to lose." And when the problem of too many users does arrive, "delighting customers scales better than you expected."

3. **Low standards for service.** Most founders' expectations are set by big companies. "Tim Cook doesn't send you a hand-written note after you buy a laptop. He can't. But you can. That's one advantage of being small."

Graham has "never once seen a startup lured down a blind alley by trying too hard to make their initial users happy" [1].

[[Garry Tan]] pointed out a related trap: founders want so much to seem big that they imitate the flaws of big companies, including indifference to individual users. "It's better to embrace the fact that you're small and use whatever advantages that brings" [1].

## Insanely Great Experience, Not Product

Steve Jobs's phrase "insanely great" means something different for a larval startup than for an established company [1]. For Apple, it meant insanely well-designed and manufactured products. For an early startup, "it's not the product that should be insanely great, but the experience of being your user. The product is just one component of that."

You can give users an insanely great experience with an early, incomplete, buggy product if you make up the difference with attentiveness [1]. This isn't just a growth hack -- it's a necessary part of the feedback loop that makes the product good. "The feedback you get from engaging directly with your earliest users will be the best you ever get."

## The Contained Fire Strategy

Starting with a deliberately narrow market builds intensity before expanding -- "like keeping a fire contained at first to get it really hot before adding more logs" [1].

Facebook started as Harvard-only. Mark Zuckerberg told Graham at Startup School that "while it was a lot of work creating course lists for each school, doing that made students feel the site was their natural home" [1]. After Harvard, it remained college-specific for a long time before opening to everyone.

Among early adopter companies, other startups are often the best: "They're more open to new things both by nature and because, having just been started, they haven't made all their choices yet" [1]. YC's scale means B2B startups have "an instant market of hundreds of other startups ready at hand."

## Pulling a Meraki (Hardware)

For hardware startups, the equivalent of doing things that don't scale is assembling products yourself [1]. Meraki's founders (Robert Morris's grad students) assembled their own routers. Pebble assembled the first several hundred watches by hand -- and "if they hadn't gone through that phase, they probably wouldn't have sold $10 million worth of watches" on Kickstarter [1].

Building your own hardware teaches things you'd never learn otherwise. [[Eric Migicovsky]] of Pebble learned "how valuable it was to source good screws" [1]. More fundamentally, "you can tweak the design faster when you're the factory."

## The Consulting Model

For B2B startups, YC sometimes advises taking over-engagement to an extreme: "pick a single user and act as if they were consultants building something just for that one user" [1]. The initial user serves as the mold; keep tweaking until you fit their needs perfectly. The code is usually reusable, and whatever isn't "will be a small price to start out certain that you've reached the bottom of the well."

The key rule: consulting is safe as long as you're not being paid hourly for it [1]. As a product company being extra attentive, customers are grateful. But "when they start paying you by the hour -- they expect you to do everything."

At Viaweb, Graham and Morris used their own software on behalf of merchants. "We felt pretty lame at the time. Instead of organizing big strategic e-commerce partnerships, we were trying to sell luggage and pens and men's shirts" [1]. But it taught them exactly how merchants would experience their software, creating near-instantaneous feedback loops.

## Be Your Software

An even more extreme variant: when you have few users, do by hand what you plan to automate later [1]. This lets you launch faster and build muscle memory. Stripe delivered "instant" merchant accounts by manually signing users up for traditional accounts behind the scenes.

"It would be a little frightening to be solving users' problems in a way that wasn't yet automatic, but less frightening than the far more common case of having something automatic that doesn't yet solve anyone's problems" [1].

## Release Early, Iterate Constantly

From "The Hardest Lessons for Startups to Learn," Graham's number-one repeated advice: "Get a version 1 out fast, then improve it based on users' reactions" [2]. Release something minimal, not buggy. "Users hate bugs, but they don't seem to mind a minimal version 1, if there's more coming soon" [2].

Graham has "seen a lot of startups die because they were too slow to release stuff, and none because they were too quick" [2]. Wufoo released their form-builder before the underlying database even worked: "You can't even drive the thing yet, but 83,000 people came to sit in the driver's seat." They discovered Linux users wanted less Flash, saving them from embedding the mistake deeper [2].

After release, "pump out features" -- defined as "one quantum of making users' lives better" [2]. Continuous improvement is itself a form of marketing: "Users love a site that's constantly improving." Responsiveness to user feedback generates fanatical loyalty because "customers are used to companies ignoring them" [2].

If your product seems finished, there are two explanations: "(a) it is finished, or (b) you lack imagination. Experience suggests (b) is a thousand times more likely" [2].

## Capital-Efficient Building

[[Aaron King]] of Snapdocs demonstrates early-stage tactics applied over years [5]. Snapdocs started as a side project, had customers and revenue before YC, spent only about $1M of its seed round, and reached $5M revenue run rate before raising a Series A three years later. See also [[Financial Survival]] for more on capital efficiency.

King's approach to [[Product Development]]: heavy customer involvement, spending time in customers' offices watching them work, keeping prospects in the loop [5]. "Some of the best feedback came from people who had chosen to not yet work with us. Responding to that feedback with a killer feature was a great way to ultimately get them on board." Snapdocs shipped code daily, sometimes multiple times per day [5].

Capital constraints forced ruthless prioritization. "We couldn't afford to build features that weren't essential" [5]. This avoided the common trap of building products no one wanted. King's reflection: "I've learned that you should take the same approach even when you do have the capital to be less disciplined" [5].

## What Doesn't Work: Big Launches and Partnerships

Graham is blunt about two common early-stage tactics that usually fail [2]:

**Big launches.** "Think of some successful startups. How many of their launches do you remember?" Founders who believe in big launches think their product is so great everyone will immediately sign up. "But even if what you're building really is great, getting users will always be a gradual process." There may be an inverse correlation between launch magnitude and success -- the only launches Graham remembers are "famous flops like the Segway and Google Wave" [2].

**Partnerships.** "They don't work for startups in general, but they especially don't work as a way to get growth started" [2]. Six months after signing a partnership, founders all say: "That was way more work than we expected, and we ended up getting practically nothing out of it."

## Startup Ideas as Vectors

Graham proposes viewing [[Startup Ideas]] not as scalars but as vectors -- "pairs of what you're going to build, plus the unscalable thing(s) you're going to do initially to get the company going" [1]. In the best case, both components become part of the company's DNA: aggressive user acquisition when small leads to being aggressive when big; hand-building hardware teaches things you'd never learn otherwise; working hard to delight a handful of users creates a culture of delight.

## References

1. [Do Things That Don't Scale](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/96-do-things-that-don-t-scale) — [[Paul Graham]]
2. [The Hardest Lessons for Startups to Learn](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/8w-the-hardest-lessons-for-startups-to-learn) — [[Paul Graham]]
3. [Billionaires Build](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/9M-billionaires-build) — [[Paul Graham]]
4. [A Student's Guide to Startups](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/93-a-student-s-guide-to-startups) — [[Paul Graham]]
5. [Snapdocs' Aaron King on Navigating Market Cycles](https://www.ycombinator.com/library/Gu-snapdocs-aaron-king-on-navigating-market-cycles) — [[Aaron King]]

title: Series A Pitch and Deck
body:
# Series A Pitch and Deck

[[Janelle Tam]], YC's Series A Program Manager, compiled feedback from hundreds of hours working 1:1 with founders to workshop pitches and decks [1]. "Every single piece of feedback given here is something we've told one of our founders internally" [1].

## Purpose of a Series A Pitch

"The purpose of a Series A pitch is to show investors why they should invest in your business" [1]. It provides a clear overview of what you have built, then paints a picture of where it could go and why raising money will help [1].

Six principles unpacked [1]:

1. **"Why they should invest"**: This is an investor pitch, not a customer pitch. Good pitches explain the problem customers face, show how the product solves it, and demonstrate why this is a good business.

2. **"Clear and concise"**: "Think of investor attention as a limited resource." Use plain English. Avoid jargon. Where jargon is necessary, define it on first use.

3. **"Overview"**: The goal is to "tantalize investors with enough information to keep them interested." Leave room for questions. Pitches should be 15-20 minutes, setting up a 40-45 minute discussion. "If investors aren't asking questions or engaging at all, that's a red flag -- it likely means your slides are too complicated."

4. **"Of the business you've built"**: Start with what exists. "It's harder to raise an A based purely on promise."

5. **"Paint a picture of where it's going"**: Show how present reality trends toward the vision. "Once you've established that you've already built something impressive, then you have the credibility you need to show how it could grow into a massive company."

6. **"Why raising money will help"**: The deck should make clear "that the obstacle to your growth is actually money (not incompetence, poor product quality, a lack of product-market fit, etc.)."

## The Starting Exercise: Fundraising Vertebrae

1. Write your narrative as 10-15 bullet points -- the "fundraising vertebrae" [1].
2. Under each bullet, list data points as evidence [1].
3. Build the deck: each bullet becomes one slide. The bullet is the title; the data points are the content. "If you're trying to make more than one point per slide, your slide is too complicated" [1]. This means 10-15 slides, excluding appendix.
4. Write out all possible investor questions and prepare clear, data-backed answers [1].

See also [[Pitch Deck Design]] for [[Kevin Hale]]'s visual design principles (legible, simple, obvious).

## Deck Structure

### Title (1 slide)
Company name/logo and a one-sentence description [1]. Requirements:
- **Concrete:** "We build and operate robot greenhouses" not "Reinventing agriculture."
- **Layperson-friendly:** "Help people quit smoking" not "Creating behavior change among nicotine addicts."
- **Specific to what YOU do:** "We make plant-based cheese" not "Plant-based dairy."

### Traction Teaser (1 slide)
Place this early. "Putting this slide early grabs the investor's attention and convinces them that your presentation is worth listening closely to" [1]. Provides a smooth transition from one-liner to problem statement.

### Problem (1 slide)
How the world currently works for customers and what is wrong with it [1].

- **Solve YOUR specific problem.** Bad: "Americans spend $400B on mental health problems." Good: "x Americans need therapy but can't pay for it."
